Thank you for the example with pseudo code. Your situation makes perfect sense
now.

Fundamentally, `furl.url` will not always exactly reproduce the URL it was fed,
like my prior example:

```
>>> furl('https://www.google.com/a b/?a=a b').url
'https://www.google.com/a%20b/?a=a+b'
```

In all these scenarios, the hash of `furl(url).url` will differ from the hash of
the original URL, `url`.

While as per RFC 3986 `/` and `:` are perfectly valid query characters

```
query         = *( pchar / "/" / "?" )
pchar         = unreserved / pct-encoded / sub-delims / ":" / "@"
pct-encoded   = "%" HEXDIG HEXDIG
unreserved    = ALPHA / DIGIT / "-" / "." / "_" / "~"
sub-delims    = "!" / "$" / "&" / "'" / "(" / ")"
              / "*" / "+" / "," / ";" / "="
```

furl's decision not to encode them clearly leads to problems.

I'll change furl's encoding scheme to percent encode sub-delim characters, too.

Looking at asyncio, it appears to be doing some kind of batching similar to what's seen in this PR.  The implementation is a bit simpler.   It simply computes the length of the ready queue when it starts scheduling and only schedules that many tasks--regardless of whether or not more tasks get added to the queue in the process.   I think I like that better (you don't need to manage two separate queue objects).

asyncio also includes some code to check for slow callbacks using timers.  However, that code is only enabled if it's running in debug mode.

Thanks for your comments.

As far as I know people have used three "solutions" for this:

  1. Wrapping: Creating a numpy wrapper module.  Forces the users to import functions like `cos` from it making it difficult to reuse code
  2. Black Magic: Monkey patching numpy to do what is proposed in (1) but automatically. Works most of the time but t can be dangerous.
  3. Ignoring it. Taking the magnitude and giving the output. This is wrong but has a funny story related. For example, it was said that Pint does not support numpy.cos because `numpy.cos([1, 2, 3] * ureg.meter)` raises an exception. Raising an exception is the right thing as the argument is not dimensionless

It would be great to tackle this. To my knowledge nobody is trying as the source of the problem is the NumPy's API. Maybe the recent changes in NumPy allow us to do this. I would be happy to help you and discuss with you if you want to work on it!.

Regarding the other questions we tried in two directions. See the _ndarray branch for part of the work and this issue in numpy (https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/4072) for a different one.

@hgrecco this only happens if the return units are given as a sequence, that's why the previous test on [line 330 of `test_unit.py`](../../pint/blob/master/pint/testsuite/test_unit.py#L330) passed.

EG:

```python
import pint

ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()

def f(x,y): return x, y

# return units not a sequence
g = ureg.wraps(None, (None, None))(f)
g(1, 2)
# (1, 2)
# PASSES

# return units a list
g = ureg.wraps([None], (None, None))(f)
g(1, 2)
# [1]
# FAILS

# return units a tuple
g = ureg.wraps((None,), (None, None))(f)
g(1, 2)
# (1,)
# FAILS
```

Hello, @jordelver! Yesterday I found some spare time to try and improve the `fuck.fish` function. This is how it looks now:
```fish
function fuck -d 'Correct your previous console command'
    set -l exit_code $status
    set -x TF_ALIAS fuck
    set -l fucked_up_command $history[1]
    thefuck $fucked_up_command | read unfucked_command
    if [ "$unfucked_command" != "" ]
        eval $unfucked_command
        if test $exit_code -ne 0
            history --delete $fucked_up_command
            history --merge
            return 0
        end
    end
end
```

Blegh.  I want this so much, but the time for me to do it just isn't there to be found.  

Alright, let's see what we can do collectively.  Maybe use this thread to talk about architecture, spec out some patterns, and coordinate work?   

It's clear at this point that as much as I'd like to do the coding, it's not happening.  I will have a block to work on it in December, but I never bet on an empty week staying empty.

Broadly, here's how I'd like to see slack integrated.

1. `WillBackend` class created, with clean abstractions for routes, incoming messages and outgoing messages.   Ideally unit tests, too.
2. `HipChatBackend(WillBackend):` created. Re-implements the current codebase, using the backend pattern. Again, ideally with tests.
3.  Test the crap out of that, make sure it's working, and ship a release.  Support that release, and handle any weird edge cases if they come up.
4. Build a `SlackBackend(WillBackend)` class that implements the same methods, for slack.  
5. Doc, test, doc, test some more, and release.

Notes:
- This would also allow folks to write gchat, IRC, etc, but this isn't a priority for this project.
- The backend should be specified in `config` and for now, default to Hipchat.  The same patterns for hipchat sensitive data and API, etc should be followed.
- Finally, we'll need lots of docs with lots of clarity. Config documentation is a weakness right now, and this is a great chance to turn it into a strength.
- With the docs, do proper writeups that make it easy for potential users to find will whether they're on Slack or Hipchat.

Thoughts? 

Thanks for your enthusiasm and your continued pings!  You're what's making this real!

-Steven

Hi @rishi-freshbooks, It's a bit funny how Zendesk organized this. Each `User` is tied to one organization when you query the user API (https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/core/users) and can be retrieved via the `organization` property in Zenpy:

```python
for user in zenpy.users():
    print(user.organization)
```
There is also an organizations endpoint (https://developer.zendesk.com/rest_api/docs/core/organizations) which takes a `User` id and returns all organizations associated with that user:

```python
for organization in zenpy.users.organizations(user_id=5386846818):
    print(organization)
```
If you are looking for all organizations associated with a `User` then the second one is what you want. Hope this helps!

But there's no way around the uuid traveling as a string over the wire, because it has to be in literal expression format when using serpent. So either recognize this fact in your remote methods, and let them convert it back to a proper uuid object, or write a serialization override. The uuid->str->uuid conversion is taking place either way.

You'll have to override it on the Pyro level because Serpent cannot and will not deserialize into custom classes.  See http://pyro4.readthedocs.io/en/stable/clientcode.html?highlight=register_class_to_dict#changing-the-way-your-custom-classes-are-de-serialized      you'll need to register a _to_dict function in your clients, and a _to_class function in your server.

In YANG 1.1, unprefixed paths in a leafref work like groupings - they are resolved when they are used.  This means that the typedef doesn't have a target node.  However, the leaf that refer to the typedef has  a target node.  So, it is a bug in jsonxsl.py.

Hey guys, I just had a good chat with @PeterAustinMoore from Socrata yesterday. Apparently the `imports2` endpoint is the way forward. It's old, as @chrismetcalf pointed out, but the code behind it has been updated and it's what will likely be supported. An important caveat: it's not officially supported, and only used internally at this point, so use at your own risk!

He walked me through some sample code that you could construct by walking through the "create geospatial dataset" wizard on the web interface. It basically uploads a file as the first call, which returns a file id. You then make another request using that file ID to turn it into a dataset and import its data as rows. This request returns the `/api/views/xxxx-xxxx.json` response that we've seen before.

For geospatial data, the `/api/views/...` response contains a `childViews` property. It's an array of the shapefile layers that were imported. Those _layers_ are the actual data, and what you'd want to connect datasync or something else to. Alternatively, you can use the `replace` methods on the `imports2` endpoint.

It sounds like this is the intended creation process for any dataset, whether tabular, geospatial, or blobular. I just made up the word blobular but I'm going to stick with it. There's a `method` querystring (I believe that's what it is) that has a few options.

Sounds like it's worth touching up the overall `create()` method of this library, though it's definitely a breaking change, as it requires a file to use it. Any thoughts so far or info you guys know that I don't?

Edited the last comment since it exactly duplicated a question in issue #133 .

> What exactly is a zoom level?

That's a good question. The zoom level is an *integer* between 0 and 21 that gets given directly to the Google Maps API (see [the Google Maps documentation](https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/javascript/tutorial#zoom-levels)). It is, as you say, a factor of height / elevation of the camera (conceptually at least).

> Then do camera dimensions also matter for field of view

The width and height of the map do matter for the field of view.

I think this is the least surprising implementation, but I'm open to adding other ways of calculating the map bounds if there is demand for it.

I’m inclined to agree with Hugo here.  The CI matrix is pretty unwieldy!  Is there any documentation about which versions of requests are supported by the requests team?  Is it only the newest release?

> On Jan 18, 2018, at 10:07 AM, Hugo <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> Opening this for discussion.
> 
> I'd like to suggest running 96 build jobs is a bit unweildy. It uses around 4.5 hours of CI time in total, about 1 hour from start to finish (eg.).
> 
> That's a long time to wait to see if a build passes, and holds up other builds.
> 
> It's testing 6 Python versions against 11 Requests versions, 3 urllib3 versions, 2 Tornado versions, 2 boto versions, plus some aiohttp and flake8 jobs.
> 
> It looks like new Requests versions were added to the matrix as they were released. I think it's good to take stock from time to time and re-evaluate what is actually important.
> 
> Is it really necessary to test against all those old versions of Requests, urllib3, Tornado and boto? Requests 1.2.3 was released nearly five years ago in 2013, longer than the last CPython 2.6.
> 
> More fundamentally (as touched upon briefly in #340 (comment)), does VCR.py need to make promises to the community to support so old releases of dependencies, and so many versions of them, or is it acceptable to ask of them to have recent versions?
> 
> Do each of those dependencies make promises to support those old versions?
> 
> Further, until recently many of the build jobs were failing, giving less confidence in the CI/tests as a whole.
> 
> Finally, https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/customizing-the-build/#Build-Matrix says:
> 
> All build matrixes are currently limited to a maximum of 200 JOBS for both private and public repositories. If you are on an open-source plan, please remember that Travis CI provides this service free of charge to the community. So please only specify the matrix you actually need.

>

Having one type of labels is not really degenerate - it is so only for a „state approach“, as introduced by Peeters 2004, typically used with clustering algorithms.
With a „sequence approach“, having a succession of same-type labels is totally valid. The difference between the approaches is mainly that the first one sees segments as completely homogeneous (bag of frames), while the second allows segments to contain temporal structure/evolution. Thus, A, A, A would be totally legit, if it is, e.g., the repetition of a phrase. The annotation guidelines define the threshold at which points to split the temporal sequence.

The author of [this blog post](https://blog.ionelmc.ro/2014/05/25/python-packaging/) makes good arguments for excluding them:

- Module discovery tools will trip over your test modules. Strange things usually happen in test module. The help builtin does module discovery. E.g.:

        >>> help('modules')
        Please wait a moment while I gather a list of all available modules...

        __future__          antigravity         html                select
        ...

- Tests usually require additional dependencies to run, so they aren't useful by their own - you can't run them directly.

- Tests are concerned with development, not usage.

- It's extremely unlikely that the user of the library will run the tests instead of the library's developer. E.g.: you don't run the tests for Django while testing your apps - Django is already tested.

I found even more simpler solution. Knowing that functions in Python are descriptors we can allow to specify containers both as class (type) objects and custom methods using only one class attribute: `container`. The `BaseParam` implementation for that feature would be as follows:

```python
class BaseParam():
    container = list
    ...
```

If someone wants to have more complex behaviour he can still implement it as a method. That way we get rid of additional class attribute and extra method call for default behaviour.

I was thinking also about adding additional `container` keyword argument to `BaseParam.__init__` that would allow us to override default container type without the need of subclassing.

Your connection is working, so your DSN and odbc.ini file are fine.  The issue appears to be a unicode one.  See the wiki page https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Unicode and the docs http://mkleehammer.github.io/pyodbc/unicode.html.  You probably need to call the connection functions `setencoding()` and `setdecoding()`.  What you set there depends on the Progress driver.  Try a few values, starting with utf-8.

Here's my method for setting the column widths manually (only works with my PR):

```
        @event.listens_for(self.engine, 'before_cursor_execute')
        def receive_before_cursor_execute(conn, cursor, statement, parameters, context, executemany):
            cursor.fast_executemany = True
            # To explicitly set the column sizes (assumes utf-16e, so buffer is double the number of characters)
            # Only apply to columns of type string -- find the maximum string length for each parameter
            if executemany:
                inputsizes = tuple(strlenmax(a) for a in zip(*parameters))
                inputsizes = tuple(a * 2 + 1 if a else None for a in inputsizes)
                cursor.setinputsizes(inputsizes)
```

Well, the actual problem is that the MySQL driver (along with plenty of others) doesn't follow the specification at all.  In particular I believe it is writing UTF_8 data and calling it SQL_WCHAR which it is not.  SQL_WCHAR is almost always a 2-byte value (but can be 4).  It should only write UTF_16 to these buffers.  I think they are trying to differentiate between some 1-byte "native" encoding and UTF_8 Unicode by using SQL_CHAR for one and SQL_WCHAR for the other, but that is completely wrong.  I would guess they don't want to spend the time converting from UTF_8 on disk to UTF_16.

I'm currently adding two values to connections specifically for the badly behaved drivers like MySQL:
* char_encoding - The encoding to use when receiving SQL_CHAR, which I'll default to UTF_8.
* wchar_encoding - The encoding to use when receiving SQL_WCHAR, which I'll default to UTF_16.

If I'm reading this correctly then you would set wchar_encoding to UTF_8 when using MySQL.

Just as an insight that could explain what you're seeing : sending a SIGUSR1 is not blocking for py3status. This means that it clears all the caches of your modules and then iterates (waits) again.

So you may be in a situation where your modules also needs time to refresh themselves and then you end up having py3status already be in waiting mode (sleep) before your modules actually had the time to refresh so it catches it up after its next sleep. This has been a point I've always wanted to address better (aka making refresh "look" synchronous while the rest is always asynchronous).

I think the following example code demonstrates how one could implement what you are asking for.

``` python
from aiohttp import web
from aioprometheus import Counter, Service

prometheus_service = Service()
events_counter = Counter("events", "Number of events.")
prometheus_service.register(events_counter)

async def handle(request):
    events_counter.inc({'path': '/'})
    text = "Hello aiohttp"
    return web.Response(text=text)

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', handle)
app.router.add_get('/metrics', prometheus_service.handle_metrics)
web.run_app(app)
```

Which I then run using:
``` console
(venv) $ python example.py 
======== Running on http://0.0.0.0:8080 ========
(Press CTRL+C to quit)
```

Then in a separate shell I use curl to access the metrics route (before an metrics have been updated), then access the '/' route to trigger a metrics update and finally access the metrics route again to demonstrate the response containing metrics data.
``` console
(venv) $ curl http://0.0.0.0:8080/metrics
# HELP events Number of events.
# TYPE events counter
(venv) $ curl http://0.0.0.0:8080/
Hello aiohttp
(venv) $ curl http://0.0.0.0:8080/metrics
# HELP events Number of events.
# TYPE events counter
events{path="/"} 1
```

Your use case (exactly as written) is not one that I have needed so far. This is not to say that it is not useful, its just that I don't currently use this library that way. I typically expose the Prometheus metrics on a separate port from the main service (which in my case is not typically a web app but some kind of long running async streaming data source or sink). For this reason the current implementation of the Service object includes its own ``aiohttp.web.Application`` instance that provides its own routes and it expects to be started and stopped. This is slightly different to your example in which you want to expose the metrics from your own (separate) ``aiohttp.web.Application``.

So, without your explicit code example showing what you wanted I would probably implement an alternative solution using the ``app.on_startup`` and ``app.on_cleanup`` method calls to async start and later stop the aiorometheus ``Service`` object as part of the app lifecycle. More detail can be found [here](https://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web_advanced.html#background-tasks). If this approach is of interest I can provide an example.

Hope this helps.

Hei!
I kept playing around with this issue and I have found something interesting, yet fairly simple.
Add 3 rules via iptables CLI, where the hashlimit-name carries the expiration time configured:
```
#iptables -A INPUT -m hashlimit --hashlimit-above 20/sec --hashlimit-name test_100 --hashlimit-burst 30 --hashlimit-htable-expire 100
#iptables -A INPUT -m hashlimit --hashlimit-above 20/sec --hashlimit-name test_1000 --hashlimit-burst 30 --hashlimit-htable-expire 1000
#iptables -A INPUT -m hashlimit --hashlimit-above 20/sec --hashlimit-name test_10000 --hashlimit-burst 30 --hashlimit-htable-expire 10000
```

Then dump the rules
```
#iptables -S INPUT
-A INPUT -m hashlimit --hashlimit-above 20/sec --hashlimit-burst 30 --hashlimit-name test_100 --hashlimit-htable-expire 100
-A INPUT -m hashlimit --hashlimit-above 20/sec --hashlimit-burst 30 --hashlimit-name test_1000
-A INPUT -m hashlimit --hashlimit-above 20/sec --hashlimit-burst 30 --hashlimit-name test_10000 --hashlimit-htable-expire 10000
```

The default value for hashlimit-htable-expire is therefore 1000 ms (1 sec).
As @pepoluan  mentioned before, the culprit is setting either ```match.hashlimit_upto``` or ```match.hashlimit_above```, which generates hashlimit-htable-expire=0

It would be great if we could somehow set the default value to 1000 upon creation of a hashlimit match.

I've pondered on this one for a bit.
Adding this function is a bit trickier than it looks on the surface.
Mainly because of the different data types you can use for position, orientation and scale.

For position, converting a vec3 -> vec4 requires information on what to set the W component to.
In 3D graphics you generally want w=1. But for other maths uses you want w=0 mostly.
The same goes for scale.
Rotations can also be in the form of eulers, quaternion or another matrix33/44.

So putting this into a simple function is quite messy to handle all of the possibilities.

My suggestion is to make a function in your own lib that takes the data types you are using / storing and do this conversion there.
You could also over-ride the Matrix33/44 classes and add a classmethod to do this for you.

> I was also wondering if there is a way to merge the event with the packet
> data into 1 JSON blob. I enjoy the modularity offered at the moment, but it
> requires some interesting hacks to get them to show up together as an
> elasticsearch event.

Yes, check out idstools-u2eve.  It outputs a JSON blob that combines the event record and the first associated packet which may be useful to you.

But otherwise, no I don't have a way to merge the event, all the packets and extra data that may be associated with an event.  This is because the order of the records in the file may not be correct, at least for packets.  For example you may have an event record, packet record, another event record, and packet record, then a packet record for the previous event record.

So to avoid the buffering, and protential delay of getting the event out, I didn't want to deal with it in a generic way.

Hi @PetiiMat,

That is the documented behaviour and is covered in [FAQ](http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/faq.html):


> **Q. Why do my formulas show a zero result in some, non-Excel applications?**

> Due to wide range of possible formulas and interdependencies between them XlsxWriter doesn’t, and realistically cannot, calculate the result of a formula when it is written to an XLSX file. Instead, it stores the value 0 as the formula result. It then sets a global flag in the XLSX file to say that all formulas and functions should be recalculated when the file is opened.

> This is the method recommended in the Excel documentation and in general it works fine with spreadsheet applications. However, applications that don’t have a facility to calculate formulas, such as Excel Viewer, or several mobile applications, will only display the 0 results.

> If required, it is also possible to specify the calculated result of the formula using the optional `value` parameter in `write_formula()`:

> ```python
worksheet.write_formula('A1', '=2+2', num_format, 4)
```

Regards,

John

It looks like the issue is in parsing, not visitation. The parse tree doesn't include `recipient` or `bot_user` nodes:
```
In [11]: print tree
<Node called "expression" matching "@praisebot @cmason">
    <Node called "user" matching "@praisebot">
        <Node matching "@">
        <RegexNode matching "praisebot">
    <RegexNode called "_" matching " ">
    <Node called "user" matching "@cmason">
        <Node matching "@">
        <RegexNode matching "cmason">
```

I think this is caused by how the grammar itself is parsed:
```
In [16]: grammar.default_rule
Out[16]: <Sequence expression = user _ user at 0x4604885504>
In [17]: grammar.items()
Out[17]: 
[('expression', <Sequence expression = user _ user at 0x4605413728>),
 ('recipient', <Sequence user = "@" ~"[a-z0-9_-]+"i at 0x4605111504>),
 ('_', <Regex _ = ~"\s+" at 0x4605604480>),
 ('user', <Sequence user = "@" ~"[a-z0-9_-]+"i at 0x4605111504>),
 ('bot_user', <Sequence user = "@" ~"[a-z0-9_-]+"i at 0x4605111504>)]
```
See [the call to `_resolve_refs`](https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious/blob/545806c7d25a9548f0d9d2f2e7f491d260de160e/parsimonious/grammar.py#L398) in [`RuleVisitor.visit_rules`](https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious/blob/545806c7d25a9548f0d9d2f2e7f491d260de160e/parsimonious/grammar.py#L452)

One hacky way to fix this is to add zero-length pieces to the rules to prevent the `LazyReferences` from being resolved by reference:
```python
In [24]: %cpaste
Pasting code; enter '--' alone on the line to stop or use Ctrl-D.
:from parsimonious import NodeVisitor
:from parsimonious.grammar import Grammar
:
:grammar = Grammar("""
:       expression = bot_user _ recipient
:       bot_user   = user ""
:       recipient  = user ""
:       user       = "@" ~"[a-z0-9_-]+"i
:       _          = ~"\s+"
:""")
:
:tree = grammar.parse("@praisebot @cmason")
:
:class Visitor(NodeVisitor):
:       def visit_bot_user(self, bot_user, children):
:               print("bot_user {}".format(bot_user.text))
:
:       def visit_recipient(self, recipient, children):
:               print("recipient {}".format(recipient.text))
:
:       def generic_visit(self, node, children):
:               print("visit {}".format(node.text))
:

In flight requests are not automatically canceled when the block exits. The ThreadPoolExecutor is shutdown:

https://github.com/ross/requests-futures/blob/5ebe6c135486bd36f919ab6aa2bf5b0b774ea0ed/requests_futures/sessions.py#L98-L99

Shutdown waits for things to complete (by default) https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#concurrent.futures.Executor.shutdown

> If wait is True then this method will not return until all the pending futures are done executing and the resources associated with the executor have been freed. If wait is False then this method will return immediately and the resources associated with the executor will be freed when all pending futures are done executing. Regardless of the value of wait, the entire Python program will not exit until all pending futures are done executing.

I took a look at the DB calls, and I didn't find any that are dependent upon the order of columns (all of our Row objects are dereferenced with strings, not integers). So I guess my philosophy has been that our code should be insensitive to ordering, and upgraded DBs are allowed to have a different column order than "native" DBs.

I'd be happy with some coding standards that remind future authors to not depend upon column ordering.

I'm willing to believe that some SQLite internal performance behavior could change if the columns are ordered differently, but I think I'd consider it a bug: the index specifies column names, not integers.

I tried re-enabling the assertion at the end of `wormhole.test.test_database.DB.test_upgrade`, and it failed (as expected). The differences between upgraded and native were:

* the new `for_nameplate` column was added to the end of the `mailbox_usage` table, rather than near the beginning
* the upgraded schema omits a newline after the last column, the native schema includes that newline
* the upgraded schema doesn't modify the comment in the `version` table (`--contains one row, set to 2` vs `3`)
* the order of the CREATE INDEX statements has changed

Using `self.assertEqual` is a pretty sad way to compare the two schemas.. it'd be nicer to find some sort of SQL parser, and then compare the resulting syntax trees. This might let us ignore the comments, and the ordering differences in the CREATE INDEX statements, while still noticing the ordering differences in the table columns.

So.. what should we do?

Splitting out the server to a new repo (#240) will make it easier to split the usage data into a separate DB from the operational data (which includes all the active nameplates, mailboxes, and messages). With that complete, it might be easier to completely wipe the operational DB each time the server is upgraded, instead of trying to upgrade the DB in place, with the rule being that you must wait for the server to be idle before you upgrade it.

But, it'd be safer to enable upgrades. I can imagine using the second approach you described, so `upgrade-v2-to-v3.sql` would rename the old tables, create the new ones (with the same column order as `v3.sql`), then copy over all the rows, then delete the old ones. Does SQL provide enough control to do this?

You might consider having a separate object which implements the cleanup code, and have the main `tqdm.__del__` invoke a method on it, rather than looking carefully at `self`. Or, have that other object create a `weakref` pointing at the `tqdm` object, and react to the weakref's callback. The cleanup object would need a second copy of some of the state (maybe just the file descriptor? but maybe also the "total size" data, to figure out what to write in that last line), but it wouldn't be in the middle of being destroyed when it accesses that data. Or maybe just have the outer tqdm object have no state: everything lives on some inner object, all method calls get passed through. Only the application code references the outer object, so a `weakref` on it (held by the inner object) can discover when it gets dropped, and then can clean up without racing with object destruction.

Anyways, for this ticket, I think the fix will be to bump our dependency to the current version (4.14.0). I'll make a patch for that in a minute. Thanks to everyone for digging into this one!

http://chase-seibert.github.io/blog/2013/08/03/diagnosing-memory-leaks-python.html

Because it is a long running process you can make it to dump Python object statistics to a log file regularly.

I suggest you set up a Python debug build and use Heapy. If Heapy/objgraph doesn't report leaks, the leak is most likely on the driver/application side and not within the Python code.

It turns out that the boto3/botocore code being called by ``result_fixtures.get_boto3_resource_model()`` is actually making an HTTP request to 169.254.169.254, the AWS instance metadata service, when running in Travis. This didn't seem to be happening when running locally. This happens when it creates the ``Session`` object.

So the boto3 ``Session.resource()`` calls ``botocore.session.Session.create_client()`` which, [if aws_secret_access_key is None](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/1.3.23/botocore/session.py#L788), calls [Session.get_credentials()](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/1.3.23/botocore/session.py#L423). That in turn calls ``self._components.get_component('credential_provider').load_credentials()``

[provider.load()](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/1.3.23/botocore/credentials.py#L874) for the first provider in Travis appears to go to [InstanceMetadataProvider.load()](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/1.3.23/botocore/credentials.py#L342]. This goes to ``fetcher.retrieve_iam_role_credentials()`` which is [utils.InstanceMetadataFetcher](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/1.3.23/botocore/utils.py#L157).

So... I think the real question is that InstanceMetadataProvider seems to be either not loading/being loaded in my local test (on phoenix), or is somehow exiting before pytest-blockage catches the request???

The key appears to be [here](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/1.3.23/botocore/credentials.py#L64) where ``create_credential_resolver()`` adds the metadata provider. There's some stuff that comes from session config variables.

So I *think* that what's going on here is, based on that list in that function, is that the instance metadata provider is never getting called locally. I should add some instrumentation to my local botocore code to confirm this.

Another way around it might be that the [docstring](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/1.3.23/botocore/credentials.py#L37) for ``create_credential_resolver()`` says, "Create a default credential resolver." Maybe I can somehow specify a non-default resolver?

Furthermore, am I sure that my function will even work without credentials?

Alternatively, we can extract the part of `wsgi.py` that sets `sys.path` into its own script, say `wsgi_requirements.py` which is bundled along with `wsgi.py`, and used by `wsgi.py` as well. For non-wsgi scripts, using the packaged requirements would simply be an `import wsgi_requirements` statement. It's still not completely transparent, but simpler.

I'll also reconsider the `serverless-python-requirements` approach of putting requirements in the package root, perhaps simply throwing an error if there are file name conflicts will suffice to avoid any unexpected behaviour.

If this is the case, you could try to convince the server to re-generate the `mime_content` field. If you're able to edit the body of the email, that may trigger it. You shouldn't convert `g` to a `PyPDF2` object. Just read the bytes from the file directly, like you did for the Excel file.

`FileAttachment` doesn't care about the file type. It just wants a `bytes` object. If the receiving end is having trouble opening the file, you may have to set the `content_type` attribute explicitly. We try to guess the mimetype of the file from the `name` attribute, but that may fail.

I have customized the Admin change_view to provide additional functionality, for example a `publish` method on an `Article` model. What my view does is to start a transaction, make the form save all its fields into the DB (form.save(commit=True)) and, then calls the `publish` method with the updated state. Thus, the `publish` method receives the potential final state before publication, it validates it (at the model level) and can further modify it. If validation fails, the transaction is aborted, if it succeeds it is confirmed. In summary, this view provides a mixed *save & publish* functionality.

In this setting if the `publish` method needs to know the initial value of some field, I should provide as a parameter the form initial data dictionary. With M2M dirtyfields, the initial state of every field would be implicitly passed and it wouldn't have the meaning "dirty" but "changed in the current transaction".

What do you think about it? Any advice?

@thclark Thanks for your interest. Since the most refactoring is done (flask-restless got partially integrated inside flask-restplus), I will try to prepare several pull requests merging my "fork" into flask-restplus. I expect the following features will be added separately:

* marshmallow Schema support (generic and sqlalchemy) - it have to resolve the [`__deepcopy__` issue](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/blob/master/flask_restplus_patched/model.py#L10) one way or another.
* webargs support with extra sugaring for [POST "forms"](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/blob/master/flask_restplus_patched/parameters.py#L28) and [PATCH JSON support](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/blob/master/flask_restplus_patched/parameters.py#L38)
* extra syntax sugar featuring:
  * [`response` decorator](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/blob/master/flask_restplus_patched/namespace.py#L84) which automatically document [HTTPError codes](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/blob/master/flask_restplus_patched/model.py#L25)
  * [`resolve_object` decorator](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/blob/master/flask_restplus_patched/namespace.py#L30) helping to get, for example, instances of an object by id, which is useful when you use more decorators, which need access to an object (in my case, [permissions checks are applied as decorators and automatically documented as well](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/blob/master/app/extensions/api/namespace.py#L114))

I have cut several corners while writing my patchset since I assumed that only Marshmallow schemas and Webargs will be used. Thus, it will require some amount of work to integrate my patches back to general implementation. You are welcome to make those pull-requests to Flask-RESTplus project porting them from my patchset or writing your own since I don't know when I will have enough time to work on this.

By the way, I am not sure where the line should be drawn for Flask-RESTplus, so in my example, I have [implemented users, authentication, and authorization on an application level](https://github.com/frol/flask-restplus-server-example/tree/master/app/extensions/api).

i *think* the issue is that when enumerating chunks (and therefore records) via the `Evtx` log object, the log object doesn't take into account how many chunks are declared to be in the log file. Instead, it keeps reading until the end of the file. So, if there's junk data appended to the file, it attempts to parse this as valid data. See here: https://github.com/williballenthin/python-evtx/blob/5fb7662506d2c39aee5a9a143a9cc47d3bdc461d/Evtx/Evtx.py#L92

We managed to find the root cause for the Traceback.

It seems that webtest registers a StreamLogger that writes to stderr. The registartion happens during library init, at which point RF has replaced stderr with a StringIO instance. Later, after sys.exit() has been called and that logger is closed it tries to close the already closed StringIO instance, which fails and causes the traceback.

The failure occurs with Python 2.5 and 2.6 but not with 2.7.

Luckily, the fix is quite easy, and will be include in RF 2.7. Here's the related issue https://code.google.com/p/robotframework/issues/detail?id=1079.

I think this issue should be closed, since it cannot be fixed in httplibrary

You should probably treat the input things and template as unicode, and then properly encode it for writing. Easy to fix:
```
diff --git a/pivottablejs/__init__.py b/pivottablejs/__init__.py
index 64c2b2f..fd70da5 100644
--- a/pivottablejs/__init__.py
+++ b/pivottablejs/__init__.py
@@ -1,4 +1,4 @@
-TEMPLATE = """
+TEMPLATE = u"""
 <!DOCTYPE html>
 <html>
     <head>
@@ -70,8 +70,8 @@ import json

 def pivot_ui(df, outfile_path = "pivottablejs.html", url="",
     width="100%", height="500", **kwargs):
-    with open(outfile_path, 'w') as outfile:
-        outfile.write(TEMPLATE %
-            dict(csv=df.to_csv(), kwargs=json.dumps(kwargs)))
+    with open(outfile_path, 'wb') as outfile:
+        outfile.write((TEMPLATE %
+            dict(csv=df.to_csv(), kwargs=json.dumps(kwargs))).encode('utf8'))
     return IFrame(src=url or outfile_path, width=width, height=height)

```

I made some enhancement (https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/12085) based on the latest code, also I did some test with single topk Op and sockeye translate model, the results shows good with this enhancement.

1) Single Topk Op performance boost
  
   With input ndarray `np.random.rand(10, 3, 1000, 1000)`,  `ret_type='value'`, and `k=3`, the optimized version shows greate performance speedup over the out-of-box version, especially when the axis is `-1`.

| axis        | out-of-box version | optimized version | speedup |
| ----------- | ------------------ | ----------------- | ------- |
| default(-1) | 601.46 ms          | 7.40 ms           | 81.3x   |
| 0           | 1436.65 ms         | 553.22 ms         | 2.6x    |
| 1           | 1617.92 ms         | 423.96 ms         | 3.82x   |
| 2           | 922.24 ms          | 400.52 ms         | 2.30x   |


2) Sockeye performance boost with the optimized topk
   
   Tested with [sockeye](https://github.com/awslabs/sockeye.git) translate model, with `batch_size=64` and `beam_size=10`, the optimized topk contributed a 3.43x speedup over the out-of-box version.

| out-of-box version | optimized version | speedup |
| ------------------ | ----------------- | ------- |
| 5.35 sent/sec      | 18.37 sent/sec    | 3.43x   |

From what I saw, the difference between 1.0.0 and 1.1.1 is in root tree `CMakeLists.txt`:

In 1.0.0:
```
  if(EXISTS ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/openmp/CMakeLists.txt)
    # Intel/llvm OpenMP: https://github.com/llvm-mirror/openmp
```
In 1.1.0:
```
  if(EXISTS ${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/3rdparty/openmp/CMakeLists.txt AND SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE STREQUAL "x86_64" AND NOT MSVC)
    # Intel/llvm OpenMP: https://github.com/llvm-mirror/openmp
```

The main difference is that this openmp folder DID NOT exist in 1.0.0 sources tree, so it falls back building using system openmp:
```
  else()
    if(OPENMP_FOUND)
      set(CMAKE_C_FLAGS "${CMAKE_C_FLAGS} ${OpenMP_C_FLAGS}")
      set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS "${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} ${OpenMP_CXX_FLAGS}")
      set(CMAKE_SHARED_LINKER_FLAGS "${CMAKE_SHARED_LINKER_FLAGS} ${OpenMP_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS}")
      set(CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS "${CMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS} ${OpenMP_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS}")
```

@pbauer, creating a File/Image-specific `IRawWriteFile` was my first idea too (moreover that's what `DAVResourceMixin` description says: "The main purpose of this class is to implement the Zope 2 WebDAV API to delegate to more granular adapters.").
But it appears the `PUT` method implementation assumes the resource is RFC 822 message, plus it does not pass all the needed params to the `IRawWriteFile` writer (the request for instance would be quite useful).
I was quite confused by this implementation, and I really do not understand why we actually need this RFC 822 support. I guess it has a purpose, so I decided to keep it, and just overwrite the `PUT` method. Hence I also overwrite `get_size` and `content_type` too, rather than using `ISized` and `IRawReadFile` which would make my change much more complex (and more confusing for future code visitors).
Note: I would be happy to refactor `DAVResourceMixin` and `IRawWriteFile` is someone can explain me the RFC 822 thing.

@buyology I made a wrapper for s3 target. Consider a customer module `s3.py` with the following:

```
from luigi import s3

import settings

CLIENT = s3.S3Client(
    host="s3.eu-central-1.amazonaws.com",
    aws_access_key_id=settings.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID,
    aws_secret_access_key=settings.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY)


def target(path):
    return s3.S3Target(path, client=CLIENT)
```

So in your tasks instead of using luigi's s3 module and it's target, I use my custom s3 module and it's `target` function.

@Tarrasch so I have started adding append support to `LocalTarget`. I haven't yet fully tested this, but let me first explain a bit the rationale behind this.

My use case is that I have a bunch of smallish files (you can think of them like log files, but they are a bit more complex) and I would like to aggregate them all together into a daily view (hadoop does not perform well when processing many small files, but works best with files that are > it's block size which is I believe around 60 MB).

I don't think I have yet fully grasped the execution model of luigi, but I am assuming that tasks will parallelise on tasks returned by `requires()`.
In my case I return in the `requires()` the list of log files that have no ordering. It may be that two reports from the same day are opened in parallel and therefore there would be a concurrent append operation (consistency for append operations is guaranteed only when the size of the chunk to append is less than some small buffer size and also there I don't think it will work uniformly across OS').
For this reason I have a mutex lock on the file I wish to append to. Before opening the file the lock is acquired, when it is closed the lock is released.

This allows me to consolidate my logs into daily views and ensure consistency on the output file.

Commit: https://github.com/hellais/luigi/commit/3e7816b1b7ec96f4b138412d1bbeef28c67d233d shows this and I will now proceed in testing this with my data.

@kimkulling, thanks!  I'm glad the detail was helpful.

If you're referring to my suggestion about bracketing the existing statements with pre-processor checks, I agree that it's not a good solution.  I offered it only because it would work with the code as it's currently written.  However, it's not the approach that I would personally prefer.

I've always found this quote from Donald Knuth to be extremely helpful:  _"The real problem is that programmers have spent far too much time worrying about efficiency in the wrong places and at the wrong times; premature optimization is the root of all evil (or at least most of it) in programming."_

I've always found that it's far more important to write clean code that handles all cases correctly than it is to write fast code.  Even when speed is important, I think it's far better to let the compiler handle code-level optimization and focus exclusively on the algorithmic level.

So, I would replace the existing optimized, loop-unrolled code with regular, general purpose for loops that work correctly regardless of the configured color and tex coord array sizes.

The code already spatially orders the vertices, which should reduce the complexity from O((1/2)\*n^2) to something more on the order of O(n\*log n), which is pretty good.  So, even if you replace the unrolled loops with standard for loops, I don't think you're likely to lose much performance anyway.

Also, with general purpose for loops, it wouldn't be necessary to check all of the colors and tex coords like the unrolled code does currently but only those that are actually used.  Since it's unlikely that most models contain 8 colors and 8 tex coords per vertex anyway, common usage is likely to be significantly faster by ignoring the unused entries.  Further, it's possible that sequential memory access might provide better caching performance than the interleaving in the existing code.

I hope this helps!

Suppressing particular errors is probably a must have since this bunch of linters will probably contain a lot of less important hints, which can quickly annoy the user. This is particularly true for pylint which still aims to [get sane in version 2.0](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/746), i.e. establishing reasonable defaults regarding less relevant hints. But since pylint seems to be more powerful than pyflakes, proposing pylint for this package once it got sane is also a thing I thought of.

Now that pylama is proposed, I'm not sure if this is a bit too much. Since it wraps several other linters, which all need to be invoked, it might be possible that this results in a performance degradation. Something probably critical for a language server, which can receive a lot of input. I must admit I haven't used pylama so far, and my concern might be groundless, but I still want to point out that this is a thing that needs to be considered before switching.

My previous statement was only half correct, my apologies. While it is true that the waiter accessed via a boto3 resource will not refresh the resource, it does clear the cached data. This means that the next attempt to access an attribute on the resource will force it to be hydrated again. Thus the difference in behavior here ultimately comes down to accessing the waiter via a `boto3.Resource` or directly from `botocore`. Adding the waiter you're using to the resource definition may be one route to make the behavior consistent. As a workaround you could manually refresh your resource after the waiter has ended.

About reporting. Frequency filter requires the whole corpus to learn its length and token frequencies. One of the possibilities is to run all previous stages for each document (possibly, in parallel) and only then run filtering.

gensim uses document frequencies. Since `Polly` occurs only in 10 documents it filtered out. We should provide a more clear label for this options.

> The main difference for npm and cargo is that they enforce semantic versioning and python does not, so pinning is a lot more consistent.

Thanks for pointing that out, I guess it's something I've vaguely been aware of in an informal way, but it helps to see it stated explicitly. With that in mind, the UX friction I'm describing may actually be just culture clash, to which the answer is of course, when in Rome, do as the Romans do. I still think the changes in behavior I suggested would be useful for the reasons detailed above, but if you think it's a bad fit for the Python culture, I won't belabor the point :)

One last thing messaging-wise, though: `pipenv` explicitly bills itself as "a tool that aims to bring the best of all packaging worlds (bundler, composer, npm, cargo, yarn, etc.) to the Python world". It's the first sentence of the docs, and as such, it sets up expectations based on these other tools the reader might have used, including expectations about `pipenv install`. I think it would be a good idea to add a short discussion to the docs as to why this particular expectation won't be met (= the other tools assume and leverage semver, pipenv by design doesn't).

In a (bad) virtualenv creation script I made I did the following based on https://stackoverflow.com/a/21263493/228539.

https://github.com/altendky/stlib/blob/0ff4031f79e8d11193e122da29273aedc0faecdd/venv.py#L56-L74
```python
# http://stackoverflow.com/a/21263493/228539
def del_rw(action, name, exc):
    os.chmod(name, stat.S_IWRITE)
    if os.path.isdir(name):
        os.rmdir(name)
    else:
        os.remove(name)

try:
    shutil.rmtree('venv', onerror=del_rw)
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass
```

Though I'm thinking perhaps the `os.rmdir()` inside `del_rw()` should actually be another `shutil.rmtree()` just in case.

You're right. `easy_install` has never had support for excluding pre-releases other than by excluding a range that includes pre-releases (e.g. `matplotlib<2.0.0dev` or `matplotlib>=2.0.0`).

Your best bet is to use pip to install your packages (i.e. `pip install .`).

I would be interested in a patch that harmonizes the behavior between `pip install` and `easy_install`, perhaps even having `easy_install` simply invoke pip under the hood. I don't think the community has the desire to re-implement the features of pip within easy_install, but I'm also open to modest contributions in that direction.

As you can see from [these issues](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue+nonodeerror+), particularly the discussion on #565, this error can be triggered in a bunch of different scenarios that involve brokers becoming inaccessible or specific relative timings in consumer group initialization.

Since you appear to be calling `commit_offsets` manually, you might consider catching the expected `NoNodeError` in your client code and restarting the consumer when it's encountered. This also points out a need for a clearer exception type, since it's known that this error can appear.

Yes. This is intended behavior. The correct padding behavior for binary erosion is to pad with 1s; you can also think of this as if _only_ the zero pixels _actually in the image_ erode the image. If your structuring element is large enough the object overlapping the boundary will be completely eroded, but it has to happen from the inside.

Consider the alternative. If zero padding were employed, especially for fairly large structuring elements, the result would eat away everything around the edge.

@zpincus I'm trying to fully comprehend your argument, so pardon any misunderstanding on my part.  There are two typical situations: Python code that operate on an image, that expect the image to be indexable in a certain way (`[p, r, c]`) but makes few or no assumptions on memory layout, and Cython code, that asks for contiguous data with a specified memory layout.  Now, it may be true that currently many of our algorithms are currently implemented that way—to make contiguous copies—for the sake of simplicity, but I don't suppose we are precluded from handling arbitrarily strided arrays in Cython.  So, if the problem you describe is unnecessary internal copies, it feels like something we should be able to address?

What Google actually checks is the `targetSdkVersion` which is basically independent of the `compileSdkVersion`, and you can just set it to anything.
It's specified here:
https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/blob/275d2360840b73cb968d2f8a9e7c833c9d2ca4fd/pythonforandroid/bootstraps/sdl2/build/templates/build.tmpl.gradle#L27

However, the caveat is that if you set it to higher than `compileSdkVersion`, then the OS will expect your APK to be able to handle things that you might not even have the API handles for.
For example, runtime permissions were introduced in sdk 23. If you set `targetSdkVersion` to `>=23`, then Android will expect it to handle runtime permission dialogs, but if you compiled the apk with an older SDK version, then you won't have the API calls to handle those dialogs.

I think that the problem is that, despite you has updated your python-for-android, buildozer is detecting that you already have a working distribution, the one you has builded with the old python-for-android...so... skips the build (as you noticed with the logcat you posted...the build date is april 5). You must delete/backup your old build files, that way buildozer will build all the stuff again with the updated python-for-android. I thing that you can remove the old files in two ways:

- with the command: "buildozer android clean" 
(Note buildozer command: I don't test this command but it shoulld work...but, honestly, I don't know how far will go this command...maybe it will remove all the android related stuff including the sdk and ndk)

- Another choice will be to remove/backup manually the python-for-android folder (the one with all the the compilations). My guess is that all the build system done for python for android is in the following path (as a hidden path inside your home directory): ".buildozer/android/platform/python-for-android/"

Note Buildozer spec: I think that the p4a's path for the buildozer spec file is one of this two: "android.p4a_dir = /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pythonforandroid/" or the same without the final slash

Good Luck!!!

Ok after looking into this I have no idea what the sort is for.  The values being sorted are actually tuples,

    ordered_constants = [ ('complex',2j), ('complex', 3) ]

Python sorts tuples on first element, then second, so this would cluster 'complex' alphabetically and then and then the value, which is a weird way to do things.  However while the second item in the list is of ast.kind 'complex' (which is just a string) the type of the second item is int and not complex.  And the function `def sortNodesByOrder()` isn't invoked anywhere. So this looks a bit like a partially implemented feature.

The sensible thing to do would be to implement `__lt__()` in the AST class.

Hi @mottosso thanks for chiming in.

Regarding...

>- Remain a single-file implementation.

I guess this in the linex of a six like idea?, but for our use case (plus testing) it does not make much sense.


> - Enable code written explicitly for PySide2 to run on any other binding.

As @ccordoba12, even though pyside2 has been officially gained momentum it seems to not be ready AFAIK

> Not implement anything; just remap Qt 4 bindings to Qt 5 (again, to PySide2 specifically)

Since the API changed and is expected to keep changing this approach might hit some issues.

> Document and aggressively test differences.

We are already starting on this and we are including patches so that users can confidently use Qt5 all the time even if using Qt4. We chose to follow (mostly) the PyQt5 api cause it was ready (pyside was nowhere near that goal at the time this project started), but at the end PySide2 and PyQt5 will end looking the same (or close). We will still give priority to PyQt5 over Pyside2 (when it arrvies) so that the chain of use is

`PyQt5 -> PySide2 -> PyQt4 -> PySide -> Error!`

---

I with @ccordoba12 on that we should work together and focus in giving more (full?) support to pyside2.

Additional to this, I think is not such a good idea to have a package that has a very similar name but takes a different approach. Having a package named QtPy, when PyQt already existing WAS confusing, but as @ccordoba12 points out, now several projects are already using it so it has somehow consolidated.
I think we should close it for now. There's no simple way for us to remove star imports right now.

@rmjarvis approved this pull request.

Thanks Craig. I made a few changes to the front end API, and I think I'm happy with it all now. The main changes are:

  *   I separated out most of the tree ring options into ancillary functions rather than having them all in the SiliconSensor initializer. Now it requires a LookupTable for the radial function. The previous ways you had to provide the function are now moved to factory functions LookupTable.from_file, LookupTable.from_func and SiliconSensor.simple_treerings.
  *   For some reason, Travis didn't like installing multiple subdirectories from share/sensors. I could have made this work by manually installing all of them, but I think it's probably a bit nicer to have the cfg and dat files be named according to the sensor name (e.g. lsst_itl_8.cfg and lsst_itl_8.dat), and these are all in the share/sensors directory. Let me know if you're ok with this file organization.
  *   I got rid of the PixelSize option, and just use PixelSizeX, since it's the only thing we have here, so the other two branches aren't covered by any tests. I'm assuming this is the new cfg file syntax, so it should be ok, but if you think we need to keep the old syntax with PixelSize, we should probably add a test that uses that option.

So, yes, by default Font Awesome SVG with JavaScript will _replace_ the DOM element (the `<i>`) with the `<svg>`. But this is configurable. You can use the `nest` option instead, which will preserve the `<i>` to which KO is bound, and will render the `<svg>`s as child elements.

This CodePen is a fork of your demo (thanks for such a great reproduction), and demonstrates how you could configure with the `nest` option:
https://codepen.io/fontawesome/pen/yqoemp

You'll find documentation about what else can be configured, and other ways to set the configuration, on our web site here:
https://fontawesome.com/how-to-use/with-the-api/setup/configuration

I found using `None` as a flag for _default_ (as opposite to _undefined_) value quite pervasive in ExpAn (for example, `None` in place of KPI names or feature names implies "do all"). Was it a deliberate design decision? What happens to those options where we want `None` to signalize the absence of information, rather than the intention to use default values?

The above is why it's critical that the receive point write cycle be at least 3X as frequent as the checks for master timeout.  That way, there will be a lag of at least a few seconds (and up to 30s) between the last master push and a replica polling the SIS for last receive point, so the location should be relatively accurate.

There is a workaround to avoid this. You just need cancel all the tasks that for some reason are still pending. For instance, after invoking Broker.shutdown() I do following:
```python
# this is because my event loop runs in separate thread
f = asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe(broker.shutdown(), loop)

    while not f.done():
        pass

all_tasks = asyncio.Task.all_tasks(loop=self._loop)
pending_tasks = list([task for task in all_tasks if task._state == asyncio.futures._PENDING])

while len(pending_tasks) != 0:
    for task in pending_tasks:
        self._loop.call_soon_threadsafe(task.cancel)

    all_tasks = asyncio.Task.all_tasks(loop=self._loop)
    pending_tasks = list([task for task in all_tasks if task._state == asyncio.futures._PENDING])
```

Traceback (most recent call last):

    self.load_accelerators()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/guake/gconfhandler.py", line 378, in load_accelerators
    key, mask = gtk.accelerator_parse(gets('move_tab_left'))
TypeError: Gtk.accelerator_parse() argument 1 must be string, not None

So the problem for me was that it was failing on "move_tab_left", and causing every accelerator after that to fail to load.

gets = lambda x: "" if not self.client.get_string(LKEY(x)) else self.client.get_string(LKEY(x))

It's going to be a bear to manage this PR while also changing other parts of the `storage` API, so I'd prefer to get the noun remapping out of the way soon.

Ideas put forth today (in order of my preference, best to last):

- `blob.Blob` -- mirrors a concept similar / familiar to users
- `gcs_object.GCSObject` -- still probably too close to `object`
- `file.File` -- conflicts with (mostly defunct) builtin. `File` seems an imprecise mapping to an object.
- `object_.Object` -- hits too close to home with Python (uber-important `object` builtin), but matches the actual noun in the API

> The fundamental container for a file in Cloud Storage is called an Object,

The line numbers are given, so hopefully they are easy to find. Typically a branch miss like this means in code like

if foo == bar:

`foo` is always equal to `bar`. So you need to add a test for the "other" branch where `foo != bar`.

I had to modify the test slightly, but I can confirm that this behavior is identical in `multiprocessing` (both the `TypeError` and the Python-2/3-specific hanging behavior).

```python
from nose.plugins.skip import SkipTest
from nose.tools import assert_raises
from multiprocessing import Pool

class CustomError3(Exception):
    def __init__(self):
        msg = 'Something went wrong.'
        Exception.__init__(self, msg)

def callback(_):
    return CustomError3()

def test_5():
    """Test returning an extension that passes an additional argument to standard Exception."""

    error = CustomError3()
    assert isinstance(error, Exception)

    results = pool.map(callback, ['A', 'B', 'C'])
    assert len(results) == 3

pool = Pool(1)
```

I suspect that the behavior in `multiprocess` may be inherited from the original `multiprocessing` fork, but I would still be very interested in seeing the results of your investigation.

@albypeace<https://github.com/albypeace> thank you for sharing your idea! I was also thinking how to limit bandwidth on all ports so that all the users can have a speed limit and will not fight for the traffic between each other and will not take traffic from other server's services like SSH, etc.

Your idea of using traffic shaping tool tc is great, but it's not complete. In terms of tc there are two types of traffic:

  *   egress - outgoing traffic
  *   ingress - incoming traffic

The tc can do shaping only of egress traffic and for ingress traffic it has only policy rules that do not allow bandwidth control. Therefore, your rules of iptables with -A INPUT will not work in general case. They may work only if you use a patched kernel (and patched iptables) that allows it (for example, see project IMQ<https://github.com/imq/linuximq/wiki>). IFB<https://wiki.linuxfoundation.org/networking/ifb> is a successor of IMQ and looks good, but it's a more CPU consuming than creating a virtual network adapter - which is the solution that I suggest. In this case you can move the traffic between the network adapter eth0 to the virtual veth0, and in such case the ingress traffic on eth0 can be shaped as egress traffic on veth0.

Summarizing, in your current example bandwidth limiting works only for the server's outgoing traffic. To do traffic shaping of incoming traffic additional steps are required.

currently the decoding context for sockeye.translate is limited to a single device (GPU or CPU), as we do not support model parallelism.
However, the MXNet backend will anyway use multiple threads when using a CPU context (for more information on how to configure this, see here: https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/faq/env_var.html).

You could also tools like GNU [parallel](https://www.gnu.org/software/parallel/) to shard input data and stream it to multiple decoding processes to parallelize.

@SuperQ could you comment on our current use of the hires MySQL exporter? Is that just a few selected metrics we scrape for it, or is it the other way round, i.e. we just excluded the expensive metrics?

I've just looked at our actual setup. Normal MySQL exporter is about 6000 data points per scrape, the hires exporter only about 1000. Still would be good to get @SuperQ 's idea of what approach would be preferable in that real-life scenario.

WRT the HTTP API: I would model it after the federation API, i.e. use `match[]` parameters. But the arguments are not PromQL selectors but just regexps for the metric name. Contract would be:
- Non-supporting targets ignore the pramater.
- Supporting targets will not collect from any collector that cannot describe itself if at least one `match[]` parameter is given. From all other collectors, they will return all metrics whose name matches any of the regexps given.

I spent some more time looking at hop, the specification and the generated C code.

First, the spec says "moves c AE character positions towards l, but if AE is negative, or if there are less than AE characters between c and l the signal is f"

Sample program:
```
externals ( stem )

define stem as (
  hop 2
  hop -1
  = 'b'
)
```

On the input string 'beautiful', this produces 'bb'.

So, first it seems the negative AE is allowed, despite what the spec says.  

It does move c "closer" to l by one character, when you realize that closer means farther in the negative direction.

Looking at the generated C code, it appears that if AE is positive, you can't exceed l.  And if AE is negative, you can't go less than 0.

Do you agree with the C implementation here?  It seems to be safe and generally useful, and the spec just needs slightly different wording.

While I do not disagree that there can be use niche cases where retaining this state might be useful for some users, at this point I do not see it as a preference, but an additional detail that user wanted to peek in (that is signified by the `?` cursor which indicates there is something more here). Some sites have keyword definitions or help tips associated with certain page elements which can be revealed when needed. If a user utilizes them and reloads to page or navigate to another page on the same site and they retain the open sate of those info boxes, it would be counter-intuitive. Similarly, if a site has a notification menu that can be be opened to see there were five new updates. The user clicks on one of those and has four more to go, then the site might not want to keep the notification menu open on each page load.

We expect that most of the users should open a memento to see what in the archive page. An archival banner is not the primary point of attraction on the page. It is there when it is needed otherwise it should go out of the way. With that in mind, I don't think it is bad to have the simplistic summary as the primary message and details on demand.

My guess is the problem should be around this code:

``` python
double = Alias(float, 'double')
```
From: https://github.com/pysimplesoap/pysimplesoap/blob/1.16/pysimplesoap/helpers.py#L509

So, the question would be how to pass a value to the soap client so that it knows its a double, without actually having a double type built-in?

I have found the problem. The change to the numba linker in #430 removed an (ostensibly) superfluous null candidate. But it turns out that the numba linker was relying on its presence. The root causes were:

1. The numba linker uses -1 to signify the null link (and distinguish it from an actual particle that can be used only once). This is different from the non-numba linkers, which signify a null link with `None` instead of a Point object.
2. The "legacy" Travis environment runs the numba linker without compiling it (i.e. in pure-Python mode). Apprently this enables bounds checking on arrays 😨. So @caspervdw your fix was completely appropriate and necessary!

I selected `mtime` because I thought that sync-level was _required_ by the API.
Of the available sync levels, that and `checksum` are the only two "sensible defaults".
That decision should be viewed as totally non-binding.

I think there's no good way of having a default of no-sync _and_ having a default sync level when enabled with only one option. Options with optional values are very bad, and I don't think that's the way to do it.

We could do two options: `--sync` and `--sync-level` where `--sync-level` implies `--sync`.
So,
 - `globus transfer async-transfer --sync` does a `sync_level=checksum` transfer
 - `globus transfer async-transfer --sync-level=mtime` does a `sync_level=mtime` transfer
 - `globus transfer async-transfer --sync --sync-level=mtime` does a `sync_level=mtime` transfer

An option that I don't like (because it seems awfully picky) is to not allow `--sync-level` without `--sync`.

The other approach of note, which is less ambiguous but a little bit more verbose in some cases, is to not support a "default sync level" and make `--sync-level` (with a required value) the only supported option.
So, for the above,
 - `globus transfer async-transfer --sync-level=mtime` does a `sync_level=mtime` transfer
 - `globus transfer async-transfer --sync-level=checksum` does a `sync_level=checksum` transfer

and that would be it.

Also:
Use same treatment for optional options for commands for which no other positional argument would make sense.

e.g. `globus transfer endpoint search --filter-fulltext=go#ep1`
should become `globus transfer endpoint search go#ep1`
even though `globus transfer endpoint search --filter-scope=shared-by-me` is valid

e.g. `globus transfer task cancel --task-uuid=<UUID>`
should become `globus transfer task cancel <UUID>`
even though `globus transfer task cancel --all` will be valid (#60)

@scottydelta the middlewares don't know the difference between `web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt` and `web3.eth.waitForTransactionReceipt`.  The only thing that middlewares are aware of are the underlying JSON-RPC methods.

So, by constructing this middleware which responds to `eth_getTransactionReceipt` JSON-RPC requests, you're emulating the behavior of an unmined transaction by intercepting the request and returning `None`.

So, the idea is that you are testing `web3.eth.waitForTransactionReceipt` by artificially creating the JSON-RPC behavior for an unmined transaction.

Make sense?

When setting up a testing environment In CI (appveyor/Travis), we first install our hard dependencies, some of which with fixed versions differing across the build matrix. After that we'd like to try to install some optional dependencies to also cover some tests that can only run with certain packages installed. But we don't want those install commands to alter previously installed package versions, just try to install them if they can fit in the current set of installed packages.

I know, this could and maybe should be done using `pinned` files but we just didn't get round to set this up properly.. for this you would need to introspect where to put those files in the CI environment (which might change paths over time without notice).

I have summarized the issues here. Hopefully I didn't miss anything or say anything incorrectly.

### Problem 1:

I'm not sure if you fixed this, and I don't have this problem so I can't comment. There was a channel formatting problem in the OP that looked like the following:

```
channels: !!python/object/new:ruamel_yaml.comments.CommentedSeq
  listitems:
  - bioconda
  - defaults
  state:
    _yaml_format: !!python/object:ruamel_yaml.comments.Format
      _flow_style: false
    _yaml_line_col: !!python/object:ruamel_yaml.comments.LineCol
      col: 2
      data:
        0:
        - 1
        - 4
        1:
        - 2
        - 4
      line: 1
```
### Problem 2:

I don't add any channels to conda explicitly (I have no `condarc`). When I add a package from a different channel, I use for example `conda install -c pandas bottleneck`. This installs only that specific package from that specific channel.

Because of this, I do not have a `channel` field in my exported environment yaml since my environment does not have any specific channels (the [`Environment.channels`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda-env/conda_env/env.py#L136) field is empty). This explains why the channels are not listed for me in my environment yaml. The solution to this depends on the approach taken for problem 3.

### Problem 3:

When exporting an environment as yaml, for packages from other channels, they are listed in the format `channel::package=num=num`. When importing this environment yaml, it throws an exception due to the `::` formatting. It doesn't know to parse the channel and package separately, and instead tries to install the package named `channel::package`. Therefore, importing the environment yaml throws the same exception as if you tried the command `conda install channel::package`.

------------

Problems 2 and 3 are related. There are two approaches to solving these problems.

### Approach 1 (Change output/yaml formatting):

The problem of the channel in the package name is caused by [`Environment.to_dict()`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda-env/conda_env/env.py#L144). It simply takes the [raw](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda-env/conda_env/env.py#L149) text package names and writes them to the yaml. Instead, it should parse them, maybe using [`common.arg2spec`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/fe411835e4c02c7e6c69421d7cf5f9fe79d39379/conda/cli/common.py#L470), and then remove the channel name before converting them back to text. When it removes the channel name, it then needs to be added to [`Environment.channels`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda-env/conda_env/env.py#L136) if it does not already exist in order for it to be written to the `channels` field in the yaml.

This leaves the problem that the import/export process is not reversible. If a package is installed with `conda install -c pandas bottleneck`, that channel should be used for that package only and not for all packages, but this approach does not do that.

### Approach 2 (Change input/yaml parsing):

The approach will let you keep the conda environment file as-is, and will also let you have package-specific channels for packages installed with `conda install -c pandas bottleneck`. I'm not sure if the parsing should be fixed around the regexp ([`arg2spec`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/fe411835e4c02c7e6c69421d7cf5f9fe79d39379/conda/cli/common.py#L470)) or somewhere else.

I think an even better solution might be to allow the conda package installer to accept package names in the format `channel::package`. This would be a simple solution (I think), and would be backwards-compatible. I'm not sure specifically where to change this.

-------
