Thanks Gael for your questions, and apologies that it's taken a few days to respond. I'm not sure who does Disqus moderation, but this is probably a better place for your questions anyway.

I only started working full-time on Conda as a Continuum employee on January 1, but I think I can address your questions fairly accurately.  Actually perhaps more accurately than most, because I was a conda user (and outsider) before I started working for Continuum, so I have both perspectives.

> I wonder how much it [(2-years-old blog post on binstar)[https://www.continuum.io/blog/developer/binstar-out-beta-now-available-everyone]] is outdated

The blog post has a "What you can pay for" section, which is somewhat outdated.  We still do charge a nominal fee for things like private packages, but this is most definitely not a revenue source for Continuum--it doesn't even really cover associated costs.  Between repo.continuuum.io and anaconda.org, outbound bandwidth served was nearly 200 TB for September, with Continuum covering all associated costs gratis.  (We're actually exploring options with CDN providers now to make the delivery even better.)  Selling various types of "space" on anaconda.org is definitely not part of Continuum's current business model.

> Am I right in thinking that anaconda-server is not open source, as implied in the earlier blog post?

You're correct that the code driving anaconda.org is closed source.  We *do* sell on-premise versions of this, and that offering is currently part of the [Anaconda Workgroup subscription](https://www.continuum.io/anaconda-subscriptions).  The anaconda.org repository and on-prem versions have capabilities far beyond a simple repository mirror.  For examples, it serves *dynamic* repodata.json and associated packages based on user and credentials.

These capabilities are built to work well with conda--rather than the other way around. And conda is the open-source, free, non-corporate, not-for-profit, not evil piece of software that I think your questions are primarily aimed at.

> What is the roadmap / vision with regards to the open-source ambitions of conda and the community-building behind conda-forge to fill in this specific niche?

Let's start by acknowledging that there are several years of history (and evolution) to conda already--and the associated communit(y/ies).  It all had to have a starting point though, a seed, and that starting point was and still is repo.continuum.io.  That's why repo.continuum.io is hard-coded in conda as the "default" repository.  While Continuum continues to expand and further overload the Anaconda brand, the most relevant piece to this discussion we now refer to as the Anaconda Distribution.  Right now, this is effectively the Anaconda Installer along with all of repo.continuum.io.  A very real part of Continuum's business model is selling an "enterprise" grade license to this software, which right now is marketed as [Anaconda Pro](https://www.continuum.io/anaconda-subscriptions).  It's exactly the same software; just with additional gaurantees like support and corporate indemnification.  It's also all COMPLETELY transparent and you can build it all yourself.  Not only are all of the recipes included in the conda packages themselves (in the `info` directory), they're also at https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-recipes.

In the future, there will likely also be evolution on this front. The 'default' conda repository, whether it's repo.continuum.io or repo.anaconda.org or whatever else, we'd like to make much more community-driven.  In effect, much more like conda-forge, bioconda, etc. In turn, we'll further beef-up the "enterprise-grade" repository with extra guarantees important to corporate customers. It will likely be a separate and paid-subscription-only repository, but it would be stictly *downstream* from the default community repository.  The Continuum package maintainers would still contribute upstream to the 'default' community repository.


> Are there any recommendation for people who want to have local repositories or mirrors to use conda on them?

[The specification for a valid conda repository](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/spec.html#repository-structure-and-index) is incredibly trivial.  You definitely *do not* need the software powering anaconda.org to host your own repository.  Any generic static host will do, even a NFS mount, an FTP server, or an s3 bucket in `s3://` mode or [`http://` mode](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/gettingstarted/latest/swh/website-hosting-intro.html).  In effect, take a directory of conda packages (i.e. of `.tar.bz2` tarballs) and run `conda index` on it.

Mirroring a repository is similarly trivial.  See for example [a script](https://gist.github.com/kalefranz/e56e8ffc53662a1724dd) I threw together well-before I started working for Continuum.

> Sorry if I am asking a stupid question

Not stupid questions at all!  Continuum's messaging hasn't always been clear in the past; I know because I've been right where you are.

Do those answers help? Are there things I've missed?

Here's the story. `with_features_depends` is a pain in the butt to deal with. Basically, it takes two (or more) different packages---one with features, one without---and combines them together into one metadata blob. To cleanly handle both, I create two separate packages in the index. The `[mkl]` suffix is the featured version.

For some reason I've lost the code that ensures these feature suffixes don't make it out into the wild.

Of course, with a proper data model, where we trade Package object pointers instead of filenames pretending to be pointers, we won't have this problem. These two "virtual" packages will have the same *internal* filename, but will be unique in the eyes of the solver.

Yep, we already have the code to do optional dependencies.

> Whilst it's nice in theory to always have a perfectly consistent environment in some cases the metadata for a package is just wrong and in those cases you do need to be able to tell conda to ignore the deps and just bloody well install the package!

This is fine, but the problem is that conda cannot _possibly_ be clairvoyant enough to know which inconsistencies it is supposed to ignore and which it is supposed to fix. So it necessarily has to limit the scope of its solver, and that means sometimes it's going to make things worse.

The only thing I can think to do here is to declare one or more packages as _pinned and ignorable_ by conda. That way, the user can explicitly state that an inconsistency in a specific package can be ignored. We could go a bit further and say: if the package _can_ be made consistent, do it; but if not, leave it alone. (Thanks to optional dependencies for that one!)

It's a fascinating idea.  I cannot think that this could hope to work without a regular expression with a captured group, though.  That's certainly not a deal breaker, but it would be daunting for those not familiar with regular expressions.

The larger question for me is centered around the rollover API and action.  Elasticsearch is headed towards recommending _away_ from named indices in favor of using the rollover API.  While you clearly could name indices with a numeric value and operate in a similar way, it becomes less important to do so, as you end up rotating based on document count and/or age relative to creation_date, rather than index name.  Does this approach still offer value if you're doing that?  It could, I guess, but with such a rotation, it seems it would be more valuable to use the field_stats API (or the new API replacing it) to determine the age of the oldest and newest documents, so as to not delete anything not _fully_ out of the retention period.

Long story short: Yes, the different exceptions are acted on differently.

The exceptions caught are acted on differently.  First, [Curator catches _all_ exceptions](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/4.1/curator/cli.py#L175).  Then it [tests to see if it was an empty list exception](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/4.1/curator/cli.py#L176-L177).  If it is an empty list exception, it will [act according to the conditionals](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/4.1/curator/cli.py#L178-L188).  Otherwise, regardless of exception, it will test for `continue_if_exception` and [act accordingly](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/4.1/curator/cli.py#L194-L201).

So if your case is not fatal from an empty list, you should use `ignore_empty_list: True`, rather than `continue_if_exception: True`, as the two are mutually exclusive.

I am sorry, but I cannot replicate this locally, there is something in your environment that makes this not work properly. You do have the correct elasticsearch-py version and the header should be set. You shouldn't have to define your own serializer as the default should work just fine, you can just pass in python dictionaries to the API, no need to serialize things manually into json.

Unfortunately there is nothing we can do since this is not an issue with `elasticsearch-py` - see https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/2422#issuecomment-91741465 for details - `requests` backend doesn't handle unfinished connections well. It looks like a single document is larger than the limit, `streaming_bulk` cannot separate a single document into multiple requests, you would have to do it yourself and split it into multiple `update` operations adding fields gradually if possible.

The correct solution here is to raise the request size on the elasticsearch side.

Hope this helps

The downside to consider is that we'd be more likely to run into ARM API limits on the number of requests that can be made per hour.

e.g. If the polling interval is too short and a user tries to execute several long running operations concurrently (e.g. with multiple instances of the CLI), they'd be more likely to run into the API limit.

"For each subscription and tenant, Resource Manager limits read requests to 15,000 per hour and write requests to 1,200 per hour. **If your application or script reaches these limits, you need to throttle your requests.**"
from: https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/documentation/articles/resource-manager-request-limits/

There are several places where we use environment variables where instead, they should be backed by configurations that can be set with env variables but also config files.

For example in https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/azure/cli/_logging.py#L16, we have:
```
ENABLE_LOG_FILE = os.environ.get('AZURE_CLI_ENABLE_LOG_FILE')
LOG_DIR = os.environ.get('AZURE_CLI_LOG_DIR')
```

Instead, this should be taken from the configuration and the environment variables would be something like 'AZURE_LOG_ENABLE_LOG_FILE' and 'AZURE_LOG_DIR'.

To explain how I (maybe erroneously) ended up subclassing DynamicResource, I'll give a "chronological" record:

I am developing a service that will (asynchronously) stream large HAL-JSON responses to clients. The service has multiple (templated) URLs and most of these share a lot of functionality: most of them accept identical query parameters, such as `page_number` and `items_per_page` for paged responses, and `embed` to request embedding of "sub-resources" into the response body.

Reading through the documentation, I thought a nice way of implementing my service was by creating my own inheritance-tree of Resource-classes (all inheriting from `AbstractResource`). I soon found out that `AbstractResource` is very abstract indeed. In order for my classes to play nicely together with the default router (`UrlDispatcher`) I was duplicating a lot of code that was already in `PlainResource` and `DynamicResource`.

So I quickly switched from inheriting `AbstractResource` to inheriting `PlainResource` and `DynamicResource` instead. It was then that I found out that the constructor parameters of `DynamicResource` were intimately coupled with `UrlDispatcher`: in order to construct my own "Sub-DynamicResource"-objects I had to duplicate code in `UrlDispatcher.add_resource` into my own code, at which point I created this issue, and send you pull request #2119.

The env vars SSL_CERT_FILE and SSL_CERT_PATH are handled by OpenSSL, not by Python. They override the location of the default CA cert file and CA cert directory for https://docs.python.org/3/library/ssl.html#ssl.SSLContext.set_default_verify_paths . aiohttp has to use ``SSLContext.load_default_certs``, ``SSLContext.set_default_verify_paths``, or ``ssl.create_default_context`` in order to load default CA certs from either the default location or SSL_CERT_FILE location.

* LibreSSL ignores the vars.
* The files must be readable by the current user. OpenSSL doesn't report any errors if the file is missing or not readable.
* The env var must be set before the context is created.
* Some runtime environments unset all env vars.

To be clear, here's what you have to do:

- check the mtime of `/Library/Keychains/System.keychain`
- check the mtime of `/System/Library/Keychains/SystemRootCertificates.keychain`
- check the mtime of `~/Library/Caches/certifi/OS-bundle.pem`

If either of the first 2 is greater than that of the 3rd, regenerate it.

@Lukasa - since we're only talking about OS X, pretty much any update to the certificate bundles is going to require a reboot, because it's going to come with a security update, which is an OS update.  So checking once per process lifetime is more than enough.

That certificate is an intermediate. certifi does not include intermediate certificates, it includes the root certificate there: "VeriSign Class 3 Public Primary Certification Authority - G5".

It looks like the server is misconfigured and does not send the intermediate certificate necessary to build the full chain. I'd report this to the server operators. (This will often work ok in browsers, because they cache intermediate, perform AIA chasing to find new intermediates, or both. Non-browser clients almost never implement this behaviour).

I debugged the issue a little bit and found the following solution:

**Part 1 - Minimal working example**
The issue seems to originate from shutil.copyfile, which checks if source and destination file are the same (using os.path.samefile).

It appears that the combination of windows and google drive file stream yields invalid results. Here is the minimum example (assuming G:\My Drive\foo.txt exists, using python 3.6.2):

```
>>> f1 = 'G:\\My Drive\\foo.txt'
>>> f2 = 'G:\\My Drive\\foo2.txt'
>>> import shutil
>>> shutil.copyfile(f1, f2)
>>> shutil.copyfile(f1, f2)
```
--> The last line throws the SameFileError although it clearly shouldnt!

Whereas:
```
>>> f1 = 'G:\\My Drive\\foo.txt'
>>> f3 = 'C:\\Scratch\\foo2.txt'
>>> import shutil
>>> shutil.copyfile(f1, f3)
>>> shutil.copyfile(f1, f3)
```
--> Throws no error (correct)!

**Part 2 - Why?**
How does this happen? 

It turns out that shutil.copyfile uses os.path.samefile to determine if a file is being copied on itself:
(from https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/genericpath.py)

```
# Are two filenames really pointing to the same file?
def samefile(f1, f2):
    """Test whether two pathnames reference the same actual file"""
    s1 = os.stat(f1)
    s2 = os.stat(f2)
    return samestat(s1, s2)

# Are two stat buffers (obtained from stat, fstat or lstat)
# describing the same file?
def samestat(s1, s2):
    """Test whether two stat buffers reference the same file"""
    return (s1.st_ino == s2.st_ino and
            s1.st_dev == s2.st_dev)
```

Now unfortunately st_ino depends on windows on the file system (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/44158182/meaning-of-st-ino-os-stat-output-in-windows-os) and specifically is st_ino==0 for all files on Google Drive File Stream.

**Part 3 - Where to go and who to blame?**
It seems that this error is an unlucky combination of a lazy filesystem (why not report some kind of uid as the inode?) and a naive os python library (Checking for file identity this way seems not to generalize well... why not check if st_ino==0?)

**Part 4 - The dirty fix**
1) Find genericpath.py of your python library:
```
>>> import os
>>> os.path.genericpath.__file__
'C:\\Program Files (x86)\\Microsoft Visual Studio\\Shared\\Anaconda3_64\\lib\\genericpath.py'
```
2) In this file, replace the samestat function with the following patch:
```
# Are two stat buffers (obtained from stat, fstat or lstat)
# describing the same file?
def samestat(s1, s2):
    """Test whether two stat buffers reference the same file"""
    return (s1.st_ino != 0 and
			s2.st_ino != 0 and
			s1.st_ino == s2.st_ino and
            s1.st_dev == s2.st_dev)
```
3) Save the file. Restart python (and/or jupyter).
4) Be happy and wait until either google or the python fixes this issue properly...

The notebook server writes a file which makes it show up in the list. If it has crashed, or been forcefully terminated, it won't have cleaned the file up. If you restart and run `jupyter notebook list` again,  there's a good chance it will be cleaned up (it is cleaned up if there's no process with the same PID). Or you can remove the file manually - run `jupyter --paths` and look in the 'runtime' directory.

Hi all,
I lurk on this project and poke my head in periodically.  Here are some thoughts.

> So, if the methods are going to stay, then I think we should ... explicitly forbid any field names that would cause a conflict

I understand the motivation, but I think this would be unwise.  Developers are not always in control of the schema that they use -- sometimes they inherit it, and sometimes it's required by an external dependency.  I would hate to be the developer who ports a project to schematics only to discover that they are forbidden from using the fields `validate`, `export`, `get`, `keys`, `values`, `items`, and `convert`, which all seem like plausible sources of conflict.  Even if we're just talking about `keys`, `values`, and `items`, I think it's still an unwise choice.

> The present behavior is to iterate over field names. I believe it would be more useful to iterate over name–value pairs.

Agree.  And this will ensure that `dict(instance)` works as expected, without the need for the complete mapping interface. If a user wants more control (roles, whether to include undefined values, etc), they can use `to_dict` which should provide more options.

> I would suggest that, going forward, the names of new Model methods should begin with an underscore unless the name has a very low likelihood of being used as a user-defined identifier.

Agree.  We can also consider removing redundant methods, and lesser-used methods with functional equivalents in `schematics.transforms`.  Below I've placed the methods into logical groupings and applied some rules on how to deal with them.

- Primary transform API (keep unchanged):
  - `to_native`
  - `to_dict`
  - `to_primitive`
- Secondary transform API (consider deprecating in favor of functions in `schematics.transforms`, otherwise, prefix with `_`): 
  - `import_data` 
  - `export`
  - `convert`
  - `atoms`
  - `flatten`  <-- strong candidate for removal
  - `from_flat`  <-- strong candidate for removal
- Other (prefix with `_`):
  - `get`
  - `get_mock_object`
  - `keys` (also convert to property? )
  - `values` (also convert to property?)
  - `validate`:  conflicted about whether this should be considered part of the Primary Transform API. It's very commonly used, so for aesthetic reasons would like to avoid prefixing with `_`, but has some potential for name clashing.  It could make sense to add `validate` as a keyword argument to `to_primitive`, `to_native`, etc, which would reduce its importance as a method.
- Redundant (remove):
  - `iter`:  use `__iter__`  (e.g. `iter(instance)` or `for x in instance`)
  - `items`: use `__iter__`

Thanks everyone! I'd like to summarize the ideas posted so far and present my assessments:

### @rjstone: Names should go into hold down periods after their expiration date

This makes a lot of sense. And the hold down period should be a fraction of the expiration period that is >= 1/12 and <= 1/4.

### @rjstone: Name renewal periods should be exponentially increasing

This complicates things. It becomes harder to track and reason about periods across names. I generally prefer simpler solutions. Thus I'd advise against this.

### @ChairmanTubeAmp: Users should be able to set their expiration period by paying the base registration fee multiple times

I would support this as long as the minimum period is a large enough period, like 1 year. It is essentially saying one can pay 10x up front to not have to renew the name for 10 years instead of 1 year.

### @parkan: If names expire, they should be "tombstoned" instead of being released back into the wild

This makes sense for usernames. I'd support this if (1) it was combined with @rjstone's hold down period system (2) users could un-tombstone their names by renewing again. This goes against the idea of having names released into circulation of course, so it's a tradeoff.

### @csuwildcat: Names that have passed their expiration period should be detached from their zone files and rendered unresolvable

Totally agree with this.

### @muneeb-ali Have a finite expiration period like 1, 2 or 5 years but remove the cost, so only transaction fees need to be paid

This is nice because it reduces the amount of money required to stay active. It doesn't address the "sleeper user" issue, though. It depends what we want to optimize for. This may be a happy medium.

### @jcnelson Video attestations could be used to provide continuity between lost accounts and new accounts

This could be useful at a higher level, as long as there's some ability for identities to be defined with human judgement. It highly depends on the use case. The protocol itself, though, has no knowledge of these attestations and thus I'd say it's an application-specific concern. There's no way to have a sovereign identity transfer continuity to another sovereign identity that is not linked in history at the protocol level. That said, one could use attestations like video attestations and in person testimonies and fingerprints and signatures and such in situations where officials have the ability to exercise judgement and update identity records.

### @rjstone Expiry time could be variable and based on the number of attestations

The protocol level has no knowledge of attestations, nor should it. It's an higher application-level detail. Violating this would remove a guarantee of state consistency across nodes and would open up a lot of attack vectors. Thus it should be avoided.

Yes, I knew it was too good to be true -- that explanation helps clarify things.

> * changing codes to use more than one character might not work with your editor integrations (like Syntastic) because the authors often use a very limited regex that was only tested with the builtin warnings.

> 1. either flake8-bandit renames bandit Bxxx codes into Sxxx

Since the discussion above, I've tested this and it works fine for me, CLI & Vim.  My only concern is that with so many plugins, are we sure that `Sxxx` won't clash with something else?

**UPDATE**: See below for a table showing entry points for violation codes in some of the existing plugins.

> 2. flake8-bugbear renames its codes into BBxxx

Grossly inappropriate, as you've gone on to mention.

> 3. we merge the two plugins? Security problems is an interesting class that I think could fit in Bugbear, too.

> I think 1. is easiest, but 3. is probably wisest long-term. What do you think?

Solution 1 is the obvious starting point for now, so that both can be used at the same time.  After that, the affected parties can discuss possibility 3.

---

#### List of entry points for a selection of existing plugins

Extracted entry points from some of the plugins out there:

Entry point | Checker
-|-
A00 | flake8_builtins:BuiltinsChecker                                                                                                                                                                            
A40 | flake8_author:Checker                                                     
B | bugbear:BugBearChecker                                                      
B | flake8_bandit:BanditTester                                                  
C001 | flake8_confusables:IdentifierChecker                                     
C10 | flake8_coding:CodingChecker                                               
C4 | flake8_comprehensions:ComprehensionChecker                                 
C81 | flake8_commas:CommaChecker                                                
C90 | mccabe:McCabeChecker                                                      
CNL100 | flake8_class_newline:new_line_checker                                  
D | flake8_docstrings:pep257Checker                                             
D001 | flake8_deprecated:Flake8Deprecated                                       
F | flake8.plugins.pyflakes:FlakesChecker                                       
I | flake8_import_order.flake8_linter:Linter                                    
I00 | flake8_isort:Flake8Isort                                                  
I20 | flake8_tidy_imports:ImportChecker                                         
IES | flake8_invalid_escape_sequences:plugin                                    
M90 | mutable_defaults:MutableDefaultChecker                                    
N8 | pep8ext_naming:NamingChecker                                               
N999 | flake8_module_name:ModuleNameChecker                                     
P | flake8_string_format:StringFormatChecker                                    
Q0 | flake8_quotes:QuoteChecker                                                 
R70 | radon.complexity:Flake8Checker                                            
RST | flake8_rst_docstrings:reStructuredTextChecker                             
S00 | flake8_sorted_keys:SortedKeysChecker

T80 | flake8_tuple:TupleChecker
flake8-future-import | flake8_future_import:FutureImportChecker

* Note, there are numerous plugins that don't list the specific violation code as an entry point, such as the last entry in the table
    * For `flake8-future-import`, the violation codes are in the range `FI10`-`FI90`
    * Is there any advantage to providing the violation codes in this implicit fashion?

* In any case, `Sxxx` looks like it _could_ work, since the lowest number that would be used (after conversion) is `S101`
    * With such a broad range, bound to get conflicts at some point -- probably OK for short-term use
    * Conflicts can be greatly minimised by selecting a longer prefix as other plugins have done, e.g. `CNL`, `IES` & `RST`

I ran into this one myself.  So far, it looks like a reasonable workaround is to uninstall python3-magic, and install filemagic instead.  On Ubuntu, it was as simple as "pip3 install filemagic".  On Windows, I had to do that and also get magic.dll in the system path, and also magic.mgc in a path that Python was looking in (in my case, C:\Python36\share\misc).  I got these files from https://github.com/pidydx/libmagicwin64
It may also be possible to extract the windows magic files from https://pypi.org/project/python-magic-bin/0.4.14/, but I haven't tested this.

So yeah, the Windows side was a few more steps than I had hoped, but if python3-magic is broken, using filemagic instead might be a good idea.

If you are really desperate, you can do the following monkey patch:
```
import six
from cryptography.x509.name import _SENTINEL, ObjectIdentifier, _NAMEOID_DEFAULT_TYPE, _ASN1Type, NameAttribute
def _my_name_init(self, oid, value, _type=_SENTINEL):
    if not isinstance(oid, ObjectIdentifier):
        raise TypeError("oid argument must be an ObjectIdentifier instance.")
    if not isinstance(value, six.text_type):
        raise TypeError("value argument must be a text type.")
    if len(value) == 0:
        raise ValueError("Value cannot be an empty string")
    if _type == _SENTINEL:
        _type = _NAMEOID_DEFAULT_TYPE.get(oid, _ASN1Type.UTF8String)
    if not isinstance(_type, _ASN1Type):
        raise TypeError("_type must be from the _ASN1Type enum")
    self._oid = oid
    self._value = value
    self._type = _type
NameAttribute.__init__ = _my_name_init
```

I would suggest redirecting your logs to file, and then including these as extras in the report. Here an example of adding a log file to the links section:

```python
@pytest.mark.hookwrapper
def pytest_runtest_makereport(item, call):
    outcome = yield
    report = outcome.get_result()
    extra = getattr(report, 'extra', [])
    pytest_html = item.config.pluginmanager.getplugin('html')
    with io.open('/path/to/log', 'r', encoding='utf8') as f:
        extra.append(pytest_html.extras.text(f.read(), 'Log'))
    report.extra = extra
```

Note that if you use `--self-contained-html` then the contents of the log file will be included in the HTML file, resulting in a much larger file size. If you do not specify this, then an `assets` directory will be used to store the log content.

Hi,

The computational complexity of the code you are running is ``O(N^2)`` per iteration (bar bugs), because the part with cubic complexity is done only every ``Theta(N)`` iteration. This has always been the recommended default approach to implement CMA-ES. You could run a few quick experiments to verify this behaviour. 

The `cma` module indeed provides a (new) way to reduce the complexity to `O(N)` based on 

[Akimoto and Hansen (2016): Online model selection for restricted covariance matrix adaptation](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?cluster=809741855340971414)

This is likely to be the best choice in the `pycma` library when dimension exceeds 2000-or-so.

Invocation is not entirely straight forward, but there are is an example to start with [in `cma.test` (line 268ff)](https://github.com/CMA-ES/pycma/blob/master/cma/test.py#L264).

In case it may help someone, the parsefail issue could also occur because the cert files in /etc/letsencrypt/live/yoursite.com are NOT symlinked from /etc/letsencrypt/archive/youtsite.com. This fixed it for me:

```
sudo mv /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/fullchain.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/fullchain.pem.old
sudo ln -s /etc/letsencrypt/archive/youtsite.com/fullchain1.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/fullchain.pem

sudo mv /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/cert.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/cert.pem.old
sudo ln -s /etc/letsencrypt/archive/youtsite.com/cert1.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/cert.pem

sudo mv /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/chain.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/chain.pem.old
sudo ln -s /etc/letsencrypt/archive/youtsite.com/chain1.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/chain.pem

sudo mv /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/privkey.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/privkey.pem.old
sudo ln -s /etc/letsencrypt/archive/youtsite.com/privkey1.pem /etc/letsencrypt/live/youtsite.com/privkey.pem
```

@kuba - I as a system administrator - that wants to offer letsencrypt to my customers.

Basically if you run multiple users on a server, you often want to prevent things from happening, you can either do it by using software like CloudLinux which creates a virtual environment for each user, or you can do other things like installing some extra code in Apache to prevent e.g. a symlink vulnerability in Apache.

Basically in Apache if you enable `SymLinksIfOwnerMatch` and `FollowSymlinks` in Apache you become vulerable to a race condition through symlinks, which allows a user (attacker) to create a symlink to another users file, and he will be able to read it.

To prevent this you can patch apache so it cannot read other users files (which in a shared environment makes sense anyway), since the challenge files are created by root and is owned by root, it means the challenge validation will fail because apache (or any other web-server or server software preventing the webserver) from reading files in the www-folder to read files not owned by the vhost user, or apache/nginx/www-data user will simply fail.

Likewise if you would run with selinux which is very strict about ownership and permissions on folders in /var/www it could cause the webserver being prevented from reading the files.

So to be able (as a shared host) or people protecting in various ways, we would need the possibility to be able to decide who the files should be owned as, or inherit from the parent directly where the file are located.

Related to #420, which on examination addresses a different topic (partly making sure that we don't create files and directories with incorrectly lax permissions, for example by using `os.mkdir` instead of `make_or_verify_dir`).

After `v0.17`, https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/commit/341fb38136418c9ba616b70103618be390d9d1cb (*Invoke getState after the server kicks us idling for updates*) and https://github.com/LonamiWebs/Telethon/commit/fd08d5325393e1f650e682ef3cb9427507ea0d2b (*Trust the server will not send duplicates*) and the fact that I made and merged #595 just earlier… I think we can close this already. There's not much more we can do. There's a thread that is constantly reading every item from the socket, including updates, which are put in a queue, and then popped by one or more callbacks on different threads. It's as fast as it can get. Calling `getDifference` only makes a difference to get updates while we were offline, or if we ever get `UpdatesTooLong`. But we'll get to that.

If any of you have more ideas let me know.
Instead `print("a")`, you should `print(update)` to ensure it's an infinite loop and hence the update is the same :)

> Similarly, you need to have the mutex acquired when calling the generator.

I don't think we need a lock around that because there is only one thread calling the generator.

I have this working with a local file but there were two small wrinkles:

1) you aren't allowed to load any local file with an absolute path in Chrome.  
2) If you use a path relative to the current notebook URL (NOT the current python path!), it is ok.  Embedding the map.html file assumes the same thing.  The underlying issue is that the `iframe` uses a `src` attribute, which works in the context of the URL.  I'm not sure if there is a clean way around this, other than not supporting inline maps in any other directory other than the one that the kernel started in, and anyway it's a more distal problem.

I think the path forward is pretty clear.  I think the method would be something like `imageOverlay()` and would take a string of a file name (relative to current URL path) OR a 2d numpy array, which should be rendered as a temporary .jpg via MPL, PIL or ImageIO's export (this filename would then be passed on). Good points, and honestly I'm not sure. Take a look at the first post in #778 and my end checklist. I wasn't sure myself what people would want when I made that PR and asked:

>should other variables be added in order to allow users more granularity, giving access to `autoClose`, `closeOnClick`, and `closePopupOnClick`?

Maybe you already know how these options behave, but I just made some examples for others wanting to see. I forked these from a fiddle I found at a relevant [leaflet issue](https://github.com/Leaflet/Leaflet/issues/5719).

- `autoClose` ([link](https://jsfiddle.net/6yfuxdjp/3/)): by itself, not useful. From the linked issue on `leaflet`, you'll note that clicking a popup is still clicking on the map, so clicking one closes others.
- `autoClose, closeOnClick` ([link](https://jsfiddle.net/upwv5ayw/1/)): this is what I think we agree that `sticky` behavior should be. Popup toggled only by clicks, not what's done to other popups/map.
- `openPopup()` ([link](https://jsfiddle.net/a5p3wrha/1/)): this won't work by itself; each subsequent `openPopup()` call appears to behave like a click and close the previous ones. The only open popup on page load will be the nth you added
- `openPopup()`, `autoClose` ([link](https://jsfiddle.net/de8d1qux/1/)): I *think* this is what you're suggesting the `show` behavior would be. Still not *totally* clear on how leaflet options are intended, so clicking one the first time won't close the other, but opening it back up *will* close others. Clicking the map will also close all that are open.
- `openPopup()`, `closeOnClick`: suffers from the same issue of just calling `openPopup()`; only the last rendered popup will show. Also, clicking one popup will still close others, so this seems like a non-usefu combo
- `openPoup()`, `autoClose`, `closeOnClick` ([link](https://jsfiddle.net/3hta0sj7/1/)): how I currently wrote my `show` option, but is basically `sticky` and `show` how you're describing.

When you first brought up splitting these out, my reaction was initially that for my use case, I was thinking I wanted both of these sorts of behaviors (open on load, don't close on click). Hence, those would be built into `show`. I have no idea why it didn't dawn on me to just call *both* in my case!

My new vote would be:

- `show`: `autoClose` and `openPopup()`. Popups will start open on page load, but toggling one of them, or simply clicking the map will make them go away. That'd be [this behavior](https://jsfiddle.net/de8d1qux/1/)
- `sticky`: `autoClose` and `closeOnClick`. Think this is clear enough. That would be [this behavior](https://jsfiddle.net/upwv5ayw/1/)

I just implemented this on [my `open-popup` branch](https://github.com/jwhendy/folium/tree/open-popup), but haven't updated my PR yet. Want to try it and see if that behaves? I just tried it with no options, `sticky` and `show` separately, and combined, and think it's working nicely! Thanks for the dialog/suggestions; I like this route.

The ability for extensions to abstract registering collections of paths. I
admit that a list of paths in userspace can easily be iterated, but
navigating the internal structure of path collection in a framework like
flask is non-obvious.

In my use case 100% of my API needs to be documented. Every single view
needing a duplicate spec declaration in a separate file creates a lot of
opportunities for me or other developers to omit spec definitions. Allowing
plural registration reduces boilerplate and avoids pitfalls.

I understand the argument for maintaining a symmetrical interface. I don't
have an argument for or against plural parameter or definition
registration, because I did not run into any issues with the standard
registration pattern in my "growing" app.

Users who are scaling a flask app with blueprints are going to run into the
same issue I did, because the only pattern suggested in the docs does not
work with blueprints. The point of blueprints is to keep views
organized in self
contained collections, but now my views have to be explicitly imported
in other files.

`update_fields` is used in the case of `many=True` where it is sufficient to only update the fields once and use that configuration for all items.

`__schema` is an instance, but it shares the fields (instances) with any other instances. So while it is fine to modify the schema instance, we shouldn't modify fields if it's avoidable. And it's avoidable quite easily, why do you object to that?

Thank you for staring the discussion, Sten. It would indeed be great to have an interoperable format. It would obviously take more to agree on the details, but I first wanted to ask about the global structure. 

There are multiple ways to analyze a given dataset, and often we want to maintain multiple results. In pagoda2 we found it useful to have a notion of dataset "reductions".  These are essentially processed views of the dataset, such as different normalizations, projection of cells onto PCs, or high-dimensional diffusion map (similar to multi-dimensional attributes Alex mentioned). Some reductions  have functional meanings, for instance regressing out cell cycle. A dataset "view" would be a better name. 

However the key structural impact of these is that individual analysis steps, such as clustering or embeddings, can be then be ran on different reductions. Because of this, we also keep track of which view of the data was used to generate subsequent results. So that the dataset has multiple views, each potentially with different corresponding cluster or embedding results. Do you envision some metadata that would tie annotations and embeddings to different dataset views? 

Misc questions/comments:
1. How would we maintain info on dataset normalization? Right now we do variance normalization with a constant gene-wise factor (attribute). Other normalizations would be easier to represent as a new matrix (layer). Should we have a convention for these?

2. How about hierarchical clustering results? Fuzzy clustering could be represented as an attribute matrix, I guess.

3. Extensions. Would loom format allow for pipeline-specific slots?  

4. Row vs. column-orientation. We use genes as columns as column access is faster and we query for individual genes more often than columns. This was also an advantage in differential expression testing, as it allowed for quick parallelization of per-gene tests. Many of these considerations, however, had to do with being able to being able to access static results from a web browser over http. Given complexity of hdf5, this doesn't look like a realistic scenario. 

5. Graphs (also diffusion maps, etc.). Again, we're fans of sparse matrices, which make for great graph representations. Short of that, I would suggest using a table representation with each row along the lines of: (e1,e2,w,[attrs]) , where e1 and e2 are elements (cells or genes), w is some weight, and attrs could store optional attribute in extra columns.

try [tzwhere](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tzwhere) to determine timezone from coordinates:

```python
from tzwhere import tzwhere
from datetime import datetime
import pytz

# timezone lookup, force nearest tz for coords outside of polygons
WHERETZ = tzwhere.tzwhere(shapely=True, forceTZ=True)
# daylight savings time (DST) in northern hemisphere starts in March and ends
# in November and the opposite in southern hemisphere
JAN1 = datetime(2016, 1, 1)  # date with standard time in northern hemisphere
JUN1 = datetime(2016, 6, 1)  # date with standard time in southern hemisphere

# notes and links on Python datetime tzinfo dst:
# http://stackoverflow.com/questions/17173298/is-a-specific-timezone-using-dst-right-now
# http://stackoverflow.com/questions/19774709/use-python-to-find-out-if-a-timezone-currently-in-daylight-savings-time

# methods for determining DST vs. STD
# tz.localize(JAN1).timetuple().tm_isdst -> boolean, True if DST, False if STD
# tz.localize(JAN1).dst() -> timedelta, 0 if STD
# tz.dst(JAN1) -> timedelta, 0 if STD


def tz_latlon(lat, lon):
    """
    Timezone from latitude and longitude.

    :param lat: latitude [deg]
    :type lat: float
    :param lon: longitude [deg]
    :type lon: float
    :return: timezone
    :rtype: float
    """

    # get name of time zone using tzwhere, force to nearest tz
    tz_name = WHERETZ.tzNameAt(lat, lon, forceTZ=True)

    # check if coordinates are over international waters
    if not tz_name or tz_name in ('uninhabited', 'unknown'):
        # coordinates over international waters only depend on longitude
        return lon // 15.0
    else:
        tz_info = pytz.timezone(tz_name)  # get tzinfo

    # get the daylight savings time timedelta
    tz_date = JAN1  # standard time in northern hemisphere
    if tz_info.dst(tz_date):
        # if DST timedelta is not zero, then it must be southern hemisphere
        tz_date = JUN1  # a standard time in southern hemisphere
    tz_str = tz_info.localize(tz_date).strftime('%z')  # output timezone from ISO
    # convert ISO timezone string to float, including partial timezones
    return float(tz_str[:3]) + float(tz_str[3:]) / 60.0, tz_info


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # test tz_latlon at San Francisco (GMT+8.0)
    gmt, tz_info = tz_latlon(37.7, -122.4)
    assert gmt == -8.0
    assert tz_info.zone == 'America/Los_Angeles'
    assert tz_info.utcoffset(JAN1).total_seconds()/3600.0 == -8.0
    assert tz_info.utcoffset(JUN1).total_seconds()/3600.0 == -7.0
    # New_Delhi, India (GMT-5.5)
    gmt, tz_info = tz_latlon(28.6, 77.1)
    assert gmt == 5.5
    assert tz_info.zone == 'Asia/Kolkata'
    assert tz_info.utcoffset(JAN1).total_seconds()/3600.0 == 5.5
    assert tz_info.utcoffset(JUN1).total_seconds()/3600.0 == 5.5
    # also works with integers (EG: Santa Cruz, CA)
    gmt, tz_info = tz_latlon(37, -122)
    assert gmt == -8.0
    assert tz_info.zone == 'America/Los_Angeles'
```

No, that's not an accurate way of describing it. `pathlib` can not go any further because it is equivalent to `pathlib` in Python 3.4 already, and that's why it's in bugfix-only mode. If upstream were adding new features, those features would not be available for Python 3.4 since there the stdlib takes precedence.

`pathlib2` pretty much goes forward from there, adding new features. If you need the new features, then you need to prefer `pathlib2` over `pathlib` or otherwise e.g. Python 3.4's `pathlib` module will lack them. If you don't, then `pathlib` is no different than `pathlib2`.

I guess I should have been more explicit. The axioms that just say mod(X,k) < k are leads to inconsistencies if k cannot be positive. So a reasonable axiomatization would have to specify what you mean by mod for negative numbers. The integer mod/rem functions have semantics for negative numbers (I forgot which, but one can look it up on http://smtlib.org). Similarly, the axioms you specify
would have to deal with negative numerals if they happen to be possible.

Also, you can constrain the quotient function to be integer type just by specifying the range type, so there is no need for the extra IsInt function.

Below is sample code that axiomatizes mod/quot for instances X, k that you encounter during symbolic execution. The specification for negative k is a matter of what semantics your operators really have. I chose one arbitrary.


         from z3 import *

         mod = z3.Function('mod', z3.RealSort(),z3.RealSort(), z3.RealSort())
         quot = z3.Function('quot', z3.RealSort(),z3.RealSort(), z3.IntSort())
         s = z3.Solver()


         def mk_mod_axioms(X,k):
               s.add(0 <= mod(X,k),
                   Implies(k > 0, mod(X,k) < k),
                   Implies(k < 0, mod(X,k) < -k),
                   k*quot(X,k) + mod(X,k) == X)
        
         x, y = z3.Reals('x y')

         mk_mod_axioms(x, 3)
         mk_mod_axioms(y, 5)

         print s

> Thinking again, how is that supposed to work? (Please actually read as: "How is that supposed to work", not as "I don't see how this can work" :) ) If Cell gets the parameter specifications, who's calling add_param() and how? Should Cell and Gate be derived from Layer so they have their own add_param() and get_params() method?

The way I was thinking it would work, given the current code which stacks matrices, was that the `Cell` and `Gate` classes would be nothing more than containers for parameters to make the number of arguments to the layers shorter.  E.g. something like (untested):

```Python
class Gate(object):
    def __init__(self, W_in=init.Normal(0.1), W_hid=init.Normal(0.1),
                 W_cell=init.Normal(0.1), b=init.Constant(0.),
                 nonlinearity=nonlinearities.sigmoid):
        self.W_in = W_in
        self.W_hid = W_hid
        self.W_cell = W_cell
        self.b = b
        self.nonlinearity = nonlinearity

class LSTMLayer(Layer):
    r"""
    ...

    Parameters
    ----------
    incoming : a :class:`lasagne.layers.Layer` instance or a tuple
        The layer feeding into this layer, or the expected input shape.
    num_units : int
        Number of hidden/cell units in the layer.
    ingate : Gate
        Container for initializers for input gate connections.

    ...

    """
    def __init__(self, incoming, num_units,
                 ingate=Gate(),
                 ...
                 ):
        if ingate.nonlinearity is None:
            self.nonlinearity_ingate = nonlinearities.identity
        else:
            self.nonlinearity_ingate = ingate.nonlinearity

        self.W_in_to_ingate = self.add_param(
            ingate.W_in, (num_inputs, num_units), name="W_in_to_ingate")

        self.W_hid_to_ingate = self.add_param(
            ingate.W_hid, (num_units, num_units), name="W_hid_to_ingate")

        self.b_ingate = self.add_param(
            ingate.b, (num_units,), name="b_ingate", regularizable=False)
```

etc.  I don't think it would make sense to have the `Gate` or `Cell` classes be `Layer` subclasses, as we wouldn't actually be using it to compute the output (because of the weight matrix stacking/pre-computing input), and I don't think they would have a real use outside of this (someone could just use a some `DenseLayer`s with a merging layer to do the same thing, I think).  This is why I was talking about how the class would have no methods above.  A nice side-effect of this (I think) is that if you wanted to change the default weight initialization for all gates, for example, you would just make one `Cell` instance with the correct weight initializers and use that for all of the relevant arguments in `LSTMLayer`'s initialization.

Hello @ajtritt @oruebel @dyf @mgrauer; I have been working on sketching out what would be required to implement python2/python3 cross-compatible pynwb.  I have created PR https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/pull/60 that passes all of the current unit tests in both python2 and python3, using six and unittest2.

Mostly this was smooth sailing, however on PR review Andrew pointed out in https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/issues/65 that pynwb/src/form/build/map.py uses __prepare__ (https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3115/), a class method which replaces the local namespace inside the class body so that the class initialization can benefit from decorators.  @ajtritt please correct me if I am wrong here.

There doesn’t seem to be a good way to make this type of functionality backward compatible to python2, and so PR https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/pull/60 moves these decorators outside of the class and uses them directly on the classes and methods that need them.

Deciding to do this is a significant decision, because it would constrain the functionality and implementation going forward, but would allow compatibility with code bases that are still reliant on python2.  Additionally, there would be nontrivial developer time needed as new features are added, to maintain the compatibility and handle any bug reports stemming from weird issues that might be missed.

Please reply below with your thoughts regarding this feature.

In our case, it was caused by an incorrectly-specified, read-only field for a outputting a value from the child of a parent accessed by a foreign key. The `attribute` used the `__` notation to go up to the parent and then back down to the other child, but it stopped at the name of the field in the parent. The result of this was that the `value` that the `Widget` is trying to render is a `RelatedManager`. The `Widget` tries to `force_text` the `value` and that used to work on Django 1.6 but doesn't work on Django 1.7. It's an error in our code though, because obviously it doesn't make sense to render the related manager anyway.

citation. With respect to this, I was thinking we should reconsider the
idea of entries and citations, and implement them as in latex. What we call
entries are References (that appear in the bibliography), and have all the

refactoring the `.count` moving it to entries and not to citations.

Unfortunately no. All RealSense cameras so far **require** USB3, that the Raspberry Pi 3 is missing. 
We are experimenting with adding very **limited** USB2 support to the upcoming D400 camera ([read more](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/RaspberryPi3.md)), but even for the D400 this feature is experimental (might not make it into the final product), since it comes at severe expense of USB bandwidth, resulting in inferior depth (and no RGB).

@omaciel Sometimes, Pulp just breaks. The breakage is infrequent and random. I'm not 100% sure why it occurs, but I strongly suspect that it's related to the use of mongodb, which lacks consistency guarantees.

If one looks over the Jenkins test results over a period of time, you'll notice that these random failures manifest themselves in a variety of ways. Sometimes, an HTTP 500 error will be thrown with a message about duplicate primary keys. Other times, a task will time out for no discernible reason. Once a test fails, if that same test is run on the same host again, then that test is especially likely to fail again. And again, and again, and again. If one resets Pulp (by calling `python -c 'from pulp_smash import config, utils; utils.reset_pulp(config.get_config())`), then that test is likely to start *succeeding* again.

If one looks over the Jenkins test results over a period of time, you'll notice that these random failures are especially likely to manifest themselves as calls to `client.delete(ORPHANS_PATH)`. I'm not sure why this is. It may be because the call is especially good at producing errors in Pulp. It may be because this line of code is called very frequently.

There's nothing wrong with calling `client.delete(ORPHANS_PATH)`. It's a well-documented and stable interface in Pulp 2. And there's nothing wrong with the tear-down logic listed in this issue. The logic is sane. Pulp just breaks sometimes.

There's nothing we can do to get rid of these types of errors completely in Pulp 2. I don't think we can get rid of them in Pulp 2. The best we can do is to try to mitigate the impact of these random failures. I think a good way to do this is to make fewer calls to `client.delete(ORPHANS_PATH)`. We don't need most of the calls, anyway. They just blow away Pulp's cache, which makes test runs take longer. We can accomplish this by walking through test cases that inherit from `pulp_smash.utils.BaseAPITestCase` and refactoring them to inherit from `unittest.TestCase` instead. As a nice side-effect, we'll get more robust clean-up logic too.
Related: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1806

This was occurring in both the development and the production environment. But, I have gotten the fix to work in production by specifying at what stage the app forking is happening.

With Gunicorn, it is possible to specify the app to be preloaded before forking the process into the desired amount of processes. By preloading, the logging happens only once. Without preloading, multiple logging is still present (with the fix or not).

With uwsgi, I have not gotten a definite answer, but I believe the forking is done after loading the app. Hence, this problem is not present.

Just small comment regarding the FKs.

In Postgres you can truncate multiple tables with one command and if you specify all tables which have the FK dependencies just between each other (eq. all Silk tables), then you don't have to turn off the FK checks ;-) 

I have been using this for some time: 
```TRUNCATE TABLE silk_profile, silk_response, silk_profile_queries, silk_request, silk_sqlquery;```

I checked Postgres documentation and multiple-table truncation is possible since 8.1 version, so it should be safe ;-)

Commas are interpreted as separating multiple logdirs passed to the --logdir flag as a single argument, so they can't be present in the directory names as it currently works.

You have a few options:
- Rename the directory to avoid the comma
- Create a symlink to the directory that doesn't use a comma, and point TensorBoard at that instead
- Point TensorBoard at a directory one level up from the one that contains the comma (it will descend recursively into child directories, so this is best to use if the parent directory doesn't contain many files/subdirs other than the one with the comma)

> On the inbound direction (rx msg over serial to NCP) we may need to keep a large enough buffer to store the HDLC output. We immediately process the received data from the callbacks so, it's pretty straightforward.

The big optimization here I think might be to decode into chunks so that the received data can go right into a buffer instead of staying in an intermediate buffer.

> On the outbound direction (tx msg to serial from NCP), I think we can treat the buffer as a circular buffer and allow it to contain more than one msg at a time. We may still drop a msg if there is no room left in the buffer to send.

Circular buffer sounds reasonable, but I think the bar for dropping packets should be higher and the buffer size small---like 256 bytes or so. This limits the size of control traffic somewhat, but that should be OK, we can always increase it later. For IPv6 packets, we have the buffer object which we can keep around until we top off the send buffer for the last time.

> Also a suggestion about class names and the way they inherit from one another:

Yeah, I just inherited the naming from the previous protobufs-based NCP code. Suggestions are warranted. :)

> I think switching the roles may be better, basically making the current Ncp the base class, and then have a spinel sub-class (which would implement the current NcpBase functionality). With this, we should be able to avoid abstract virtual methods like OutboundFrameBegin, ... in the base class.

I personally think the subclassing is rather unfortunate in this case, and would advocate abandoning that approach rather than swapping the meanings. This is a big rewrite, which is why I've been hesitant to dive in.

The nice thing about the current class structure is that it is pretty straightforward to create a new subclass for supporting a new transport, like SPI.

Files with a `.` prefix aren't shown when you do a `ls` without the `-h` flag. We generally only want to hide files that aren't really a part of the sources for the project (Like `.travis.yml`, `.appveyor.yml`, `.gitignore`, etc.), are under-the-hood parts of the build system (`.default-version`). They are things that we generally don't want people to see because they don't need to see them. Ideally those files would be hidden on Windows, too.

In this case, we *want* people to see `openthread.sln`. We just don't want it in the root folder.

Here is a quick straw-man fragmentation protocol (assumes a reliable frame transport under the hood):

* Fragment frames are limited to a size of 256 bytes. (Actual limit is TBD. Should this be smaller?)
* The first byte is a header byte, leaving a maximum of 255 bytes of data per fragment.
* The least significant bit of the header byte is the "more fragments pending" bit (`FLAG_MORE_FRAG_PEND`). All other bits are reserved and set to zero.
* Fragmented frames are sent sequentially with the last fragment of the super frame having `FLAG_MORE_FRAG_PEND` cleared.
* Fragments can be less than 255 bytes long.

I ended up rewriting data preprocessing code to vectorize words from the dataset, pad sequences of vectors representing the sequences of words in the given input sentence and then pass those sequences as X into ML network. Indeed model started working much better and started returning lower accuracy for predictions that it could not find a match for. I however had to build a custom lemmatization feature, the modules that I found were stemming words way too much. One example is word "operator" was stemmed into "oper", which would not work for me. Therefore I build my own that converted all verbs into infinitive state and converted all plural nouns into singular. That worked much better. Hey nullglitch,

This isn't documented as far as I can see, so I went to the source code and to the metadata API to find out more. It took a fair amount of effort.

'options' is a list of unpackaged metadata types to retrieve, and the members of that type you want to retrieve.

In my case I used the following code (roughly):

`metatypes = ['AccountSettings', 'ActionLinkGroupTemplate', 'ActionOverride']
retrieve_options = dict(single_package=False)
# include all members of each type, in order
unpackaged_types = OrderedDict()
for unpackaged_type in metatypes:
    unpackaged_types[unpackaged_type] = '*'
    retrieve_options['single_package'] = False
    retrieve_options['unpackaged'] = unpackaged_types
    return retrieve_options

If you try to retrieve all metatypes at once you may hit Python's memory limit, as sfdclib tries to build the zip in memory. Depends how big your Salesforce solution is.

There are one or two metadata types that cause errors. I'm raising a separate ticket for those.

Cheers,

Nick

Actually, I don't think you need to be this complicated. Block storage is hard, and doesn't buy us that much. The following would solve most of use cases and be more easily debugged: 

1.2. exactly as you say.

3. Skip second pass entirely.

4. Train time. Load data by reading from text files. However, never construct all the examples in memory. Simply buffer enough examples from a file to construct reasonably sized batches. (This should be user controllable. Note this buffering line is bad: magic number `100`):

https://github.com/pytorch/text/blob/master/torchtext/data/iterator.py#L271 

The main contribution is that datasets should be inherently lazy. It should be easy to make one, but it shouldn't default to loading everything in. When you call `build_vocab` it should go line by line. When you make an iterator it should automatically buffer enough examples for tight batching. Currently both these operations take up a ton of memory.

All vocabularies are `defaultdict` objects, so index zero is reserved for unknown or unseen inputs. Fields with `sequential=True`, which is the default but should be turned off for your `label_field` because the label data isn't a sequence, also have a reserved index for padding.
But since your inputs are already numeric, you can get away with setting `use_vocab=False`, in which case I believe you can get what you're looking for by setting `postprocessing=Pipeline(int)`. In that case there won't be any extra indices, just zero and one.

>  I'm not too worried about omitting prefixes if we requested an indent of 0. (What does that even mean?)

I read through it, and i think i have a good sense of what it's trying to do.

When we emit text, we can emit it at some 'indent' level.  This indent level ends up being a multiple of 4 columns. 

We also support the idea of having hte line be 'prefixed' with the Op symbol (i.e. `+` or `-` or `+-`).  Importantly, this has no effect on indentation.  So the way the code works is by indenting first, then knowing that the Op symbol can overwrite any of the first indentation spaces. 

So, having someone say: i don't want any indent... but i also want the op-prefix ends up being something unsupported since there's no actual place to put that op symbol.

--

Now, the reason i preferred my suggestion was that what we are printing are the stack outputs (including showing how they changed).  In that case, having the op-symbol is useful and consistent with the rest of our presentation.  So, in order to support that, we should just indent in all the outputs by at least '1' so that we can print that out.  It feels like the right fix.  Though i'm totally with anyone who thinks all this code is confusing and error prone and could use some significant love to improve it and to help avoid these traps.

I also like the piping feature, but for a different reason: I watch a lot of Twitch and for certain things (like bigger Dota2 tournaments) I have the problem that they broadcast on US and SEA timezones. So I sometimes use `livestreamer` to simply dump the stream to a file and tell VLC to open that file. This way I get an easy timeshifting setup where I can pause my playback to things like housework and then come back later to resume where I left off  (and get a free "skip boring things" feature).

Besides this, I have the feeling that this comment track goes in a strange direction: From reworking the CLI to a pretty meta discussion about the general direction (hint, hint: Maybe that should be discussed elsewhere ;) )

To come back to the actual discussion: I also think that the CLI is complicated, sometimes confusing and repetitive. I have the feeling that we could do pretty well with splitting it up into different subcommands like `git` and similar tools do. This way we could provide a quick way to reach different "modes". I.e:

- `streamlink play <url>`: current default behaviour
- `streamlink save <url>`: dump to a file
- `streamlink proxy <url>`: launch a http server that serves the stream
- `streamlink info <url> --json`: show information about the stream a JSON

Hi there,

Thanks for the interest and the report. The above error is actually from `parallel-ssh`, but both projects have same issue building on pypy. This is fixed in latest master branches for both projects, releases pending some travis CI issues.

Note that `parallel-ssh` is not supported on libssh2 < 1.6. `ssh2-python` by itself works with caveats for known issues on old versions like lack of thread safety. You will need to install a recent version of `libssh2` if wanting to use parallel-ssh on pypy, though it builds without errors if just for CI purposes. For production use I'd also recommend PyPy >= 5.9 as its C-API performance [has improved a lot](https://morepypy.blogspot.com/2017/10/pypy-v59-released-now-supports-pandas.html).

Also, PyPy does not support [distributable wheels](https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/2617/) yet so no binary wheels are built for pypy. CPython wheels include libssh2 and other dependencies for both `parallel-ssh` and `ssh2-python` on all platforms.

That is pretty easy:

```
<make session>
chan = session.open_session()
chan.pty()
chan.execute('less -F /var/log/messages')
size, data = chan.read()
while size > 0:
    <..>
    if <break condition>:
        break
    size, data = chan.read()
chan.close()
session.disconnect()
```

Reading will never complete with a command like that, so will need to break out of the loop after some break condition, or use non-blocking mode with a timeout.

> It's not associated with any binding or task in particular – binding a scope to a task makes it so that when a cancellation occurs, that task is notified.

I see. I totally missunderstood the idea of the "cancel binding"! I thought it was a sort of bidirectional link: for the task to be able to send a "cancel" to a cancel scope and for the cancel scope to notify the cancellation (in-process) to the task. According to your comments, it is only for the "_Cancel scope to task_" notification means.

> What sort of data?

My idea was for data that the _cancel scope_ could use to provide information about the task that requested the cancellation or to even block the cancellation process according to some properties of this data... but that only made sense when I thought that the _cancel binding_ was bidirectional. Now that I know your idea of a _cancel binding_ I don't see any useful data to "store" there.

> Generally the idea of functions is to be an abstraction over callers – and trio's cancellation system nudges you in that direction by not allowing fine-grained access to information about caller scopes.

I agree to not giving access to information about "caller scopes"... but I don't see a problem in letting the caller _send_ to the cancel scope some information about him or about the cancellation "reason" when he invokes the _cancel_ operation on the cancel scope. This is seeing the _cancel_ operation as a method of the cancel scope that can be explicitly called.

> So that's adding additional granularity in a slightly different way: the canceller can send slightly different information (though you still can't select on source of the cancellation).

That's more or less the idea. Although I don't understand your final: "_though you still can't select on source of the cancellation_". What do you mean?

> I suppose a super-fancy version of this would be to allow user-defined cancellation states, like allow scope.cancel(tag=X) to mark it "cancelled in the X manner", and then code could opt-in or out-out to that on a tag-by-tag basis.

You can see it as "cancellation states" or just as "relevant information that the canceller wants to broadcast to the other tasks affected by the cancellation scope so they can better decide what to do". Does that make sense to anyone when seen this way?

> I'm having trouble coming up with anything that would justify so much complexity, though :-)

First, it doesn't have to be complex. It is not complex if we see it just as a "way to send extra/contextual information to all tasks affected by the cancellation process" by doing it through the cancel scope itself.

And second, I insist in not trying to desperately simplify the overall design **too soon**. It would only deter creativity. We will have time for that. Let's give us some more time to find examples and situations where it may be useful. I neither have them yet, but let the river flow... and we'll see where it takes us.

Another use for a `TrioRunToken` kind of object is that it could provide `==` or similar (or even just `is`) as a way to check if two runs were the same. Example use case: catching when someone tries to use a pytest-trio fixture with `scope="session"`.

It could potentially provide some other niceties too, like a way to check if the loop is running without entering it. Though... meh, this is racey, not sure it's really interesting. You can always `call_soon(lambda: None)` if you really want to know :-).

As a suggestion, add a `trio.abc.SocketType` ABC, which `trio._socket._SocketType` should inherit from, and for which `SocketFactory` instances should register their socket class with.

(Alternatively to requiring registration, call `SocketType.register` on the type of socket returned by the socket factory in `trio.socket.socket`, as that's the only socket constructor; ABC registration is cached, and should be fast.)

This would allow `isinstance(sock, SocketType)` and `issubclass(socktype, SocketType)` to work as before.

I've been using `functools.singledispatch` for various tasks; it relies on `issubclass` for determining which function to dispatch to (the choice is cached, keyed by the object type). You can't do subclass-testing with only `is_trio_socket`; I would end up having to write the above ABC, and require users of my library to use my socket function instead of trio's, to ensure the socket class is registered. ('My users' = 'me', so it's not a big deal :-))

Additionally, I've also been playing around with [mypy](http://mypy-lang.org/) for static type checking; it really only understands `isinstance` for dynamic type refinement, so in this case you want to be able to do `isinstance(sock, SocketType)`.

Here's a possible API that might be a bit simpler to use: allow specifying a list of source addresses, and each socket walks down the list and uses the first one that it can.

So `["1.2.3.4", "1:2:3::4"]` would use `1.2.3.4` for outgoing IPv4 connections, and `1:2:3::4` for outgoing IPv6 connections, with random ports. (Using `IP_BIND_ADDRESS_NO_PORT` if available, otherwise falling back on port 0.) Specifying `["1.2.3.4"]` would force the use of IPv4 for this connection.

Do people need the ability to control the outgoing port? We could also allow `[("1.2.3.4", 5678)]` to specify the source port, and this would even allow folks to write silly things like `[("1.2.3.4", 5678), ("1.2.3.4", 9012)]` if they wanted to specify a set of allowable source ports (so the first attempt would use 5678, then the second one would try to bind that and fail if the first one was still using it, and then move on to try binding 9012 instead). But possibly this is something we should defer until someone actually needs it.

Hi @gdemarcsek! The sampling logic here is that if tracing is enabled based on the trace options in the trace ID got from the request headers (say if we deploy a flask app on AppEngine Flex), we will consult our sampler to see whether this request should be sampled or not. But if the trace options is 0 which means this request should not be traced, we don't need to consult the sampler in this case. If we change `and` to `or` here, we would respect the sampler more than the trace option, which is not correct. And by default the trace option is 1 in the trace ID that generated by GFE, so the sampler will not have any effect if we use `or` here.

**TL;DR** velocyto.py right now does not support STRT. using velocyto.R SmartSeq2 pipeline might perform better than what you are reporting but it would still be an abuse of the pipeline logic. 

velocyto.py logic is designed for 3' scRNA-Seq chemistry like 10X and DropSeq. It cannot be used "as is" to your protocol since it is STRT inspired.
The "poor" results are expected for different reasons. For example:
- With 5' protocols you are getting one genomic mapping position per cell barcode, even if the mapping is intronic, you have only one evidence of mapping per molecule. Notice that in 3' protocols the transcript is read from the body (5') towards the poly-A tail (3') and the position that you are reading depends from the fragmentation step, therefore you have the chance to read transcripts at different locations and to combine this information you can classify them as spliced or unspliced. This means that STRT protocol should be analysed more like SmartSeq2 rather than 10X/Drop-Seq, just by counting UMI instead of reads.
- analysing few cells will also cause trouble since velocyto.py is conservative on calling unspliced molecules and require at least one evidence of intron-exon spanning read in the whole dataset to count intron mapping. So few small cells might mean fewer evidence of this spanning evidence and therefore a lot of reads won't be counted. You could try to set --introns permissive to release this check but then you will be making your data more noisy.

We might support STRT in the future (after appropriate testing of a STRT specific logic) but right now I am afraid there is no good solution to make this work for your use case.

Looks like a race condition.  What I'm seeing with a locally built and instrumented connectivity app is that a BLE event comes in and ser_conn_ble_event_encoder() allocates the tx buffer for it, successfully queues it for send, and pauses the scheduler.

Normally the transmission completes and the scheduler is resumed() before the next command packet comes in.  For unknown reasons in some situations the transmission never occurs, i.e. there's no ser_hal_transport phy_events_handler() invocation for it.  I've seen up to 100 ms before the next event.

In the observed case the next command packet comes in and ser_conn_command_process() spins waiting for the tx buffer to become available, which never happens and so all main loop activity is blocked.

I now suspect the `3` is from a timeout in `pc-ble-driver` due to the BLE response never being received.

Still no idea why this is occurring.  I do have a lot of beacons babbling in my environment.

Short answer: set `pd.daemon = False` prior to `pd.start()` will prevent dropped messages (there's no need to set linger anywhere, as 0MQ's default is -1).

Behavior is indeed unique to using the ProcessDevice object (or indeed, launching a device in via multiprocessing at all from a script that will try to exit while the device should still be active).  The issue is that garbage collection at the end of your script is triggering the destruction of the subprocess.  Setting `pd.daemon=False` means that Python will not exit until the process finishes.  Of course, the whole reason that devices default daemon to True is that they will never finish, so if you set daemon to False, your application will never exit.

So, the real practical answer is, if you have a device in a subprocess, don't exit your application until you have confirmation that the device has performed its duty, because it will be destroyed at exit.

An important thing to note here is that your `time.sleep()` solution need not be before closing/terminating the client socket, it can be at the very end of your script, and it will still have the same effect.

I also noticed that using a ThreadDevice does not suffer this problem (still works when `daemon=True`), perhaps because garbage collection of Threads is less forceful, and close/term still get called.
